According to the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}, an entry dated the 26th day of the 2nd month in the 9th year of Jōgan (867) mentions that the Hayashi Uji Shrine of Kawachi Province was recognized as a state shrine. Another entry from the 20th day of the 12th month in the 15th year of Jōgan (873) records the elevation of the enshrined deity Ame-no-Oshihi to the rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. During the {{ill|Sengoku period (Japan)|ja|戦国時代}}, the shrine was burned down by the forces of {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|ja|織田信長}}. After the extinction of the Tomobayashi clan, local residents reconstructed the shrine as a place to worship their {{ill|Ujigami|ja|産土神}}. In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the shrine's identification as Tomobayashi Uji Shrine was debated among {{ill|Kokugaku|lt=Kokugaku scholars|ja|国学}}. Despite inquiries by Edo officials, local village leaders at the time claimed it was a shrine dedicated to Gozu Tennō. By the late Edo period, the shrine became recognized as a site for worshiping the ancestral deity of the Ōtomo clan, influenced by the work of {{ill|Tomobayashi Mitsuhei|ja|伴林光平}}, a Kokugaku scholar from the village.Keita Endō, "Studies and Restoration of Shikinaisha: Investigation of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine" (First published in ''Shidai Nihonshi'', Vol. 5, 2002 / Collected in Endō, ''Formation of the Nihon Shoki and Related Materials'', Hanawa Shobō, 2015). In 1872 (Meiji 5), the shrine was designated as a village shrine. In 1932 (Shōwa 7), a survey commemorating the 50th anniversary of the {{ill|Imperial Rescript to Soldiers and Sailors|ja|軍人勅諭}} confirmed that this shrine is the only one in Japan dedicated to Michinoomi no Mikoto, the military deity of the Ōtomo clan. Consequently, it gained attention as the "Western Yasukuni Shrine." This designation heavily relied on the interpretation of Kokugaku scholar and historian {{ill|Ikuta Namekine|ja|生田目経徳}}. Subsequent renovations and expansions led to the completion of new shrine buildings in 1940 (Shōwa 15), including the relocation of a {{ill|Chōzuya|ja|手水舎}} donated by {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}} in Tokyo. The shrine was elevated to the rank of prefectural shrine in 1942 (Shōwa 17). The shrine marker was inscribed by {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}}. == Grounds == * Main Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Offering Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Corridor Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940. * Gate (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940. * Shrine Office * Chōzuya (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Donated by Yasukuni Shrine in 1940; originally constructed in 1872. === Sub-Shrines === * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Formerly the reliquary hall of Sawada Elementary School (now Dōmyōji Elementary School). == Cultural Properties == === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022.Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 100, June 29, 2022. * Worship Hall and Offering Hall of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Gate and Corridor Fence of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Chōzuya of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Hall (Former Sawada Elementary School Reliquary Hall) - Registered June 29, 2022. == location == * Address: Hayashi 3-chome 6-30, Fujidera City, Osaka Prefecture == Access == * By rail: 10-minute walk northwest (approximately 800m) from {{ill|Hajinonosato Station|ja|土師ノ里駅}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == External links == {{Commonscat|Tomobayashiuji-jinja}} *[http://www.tomobayashiuji-jinja.jp/index.html Tomobayashi Uji Shrine] [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Fujidera]] [[Category:History of Fujidera]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Former prefectural shrines in Osaka]] [[Category:bump]] [[Category:Tomobayashi Shrine]][[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:Tomobayashi Shrine]][[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[ja:伴林氏神社]]

Worship Hall | location = {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}{{ill|Fujidera City|ja|藤井寺市}} Hayashi 3-chome 6-30 | Latitude degrees = 34 | Latitude minutes = 34 | Latitude seconds = 32.649 | N/S = N | Longitude degrees = 135 | Longitude minutes = 36 | Longitude seconds = 40.439 | E/W = E | Map country code = JP | Map = Japan Osaka | Deities = {{ill|Takami-Musubi no Kami|ja|高皇産霊神}} | Shrine rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor)
Former {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|府社}} | Founded = Unknown | Main Hall = | Other names = | Pilgrimage sites = | festival = October 9 | Rituals = }} [[File:Tomobayashiuji-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Tomobayashi Uji Shrine''' (伴林氏神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Fujidera City|ja|藤井寺市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, in the Hayashi area of the former Haishi-go, Shiki District, Kawachi Province. The shrine is listed as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} and previously held the rank of {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|府社}}. It was once referred to as the "Western {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}}." == Deities == * Primary deity - {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|高皇産霊神}} (たかむすひのかみ) * Secondary deities - {{ill|Michinoomi no Mikoto|ja|道臣命}} (みちのおみのみこと), {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天押日命}} (あめのおしひのみこと) The shrine's name suggests that it was originally established by the {{ill|Tomobayashi clan|ja|伴林氏}}, a branch of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}, to worship their ancestral deities. Takami-Musubi no Kami is identified as the fifth-generation ancestor of Ame-no-Oshihi, who in turn is the third-generation ancestor of Michinoomi no Mikoto. == History == [[File:Tomobayashiuji-jinja, chouzusha.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Chōzuya}}]] The founding year of the shrine is unknown. According to the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}, an entry dated the 26th day of the 2nd month in the 9th year of Jōgan (867) mentions that the Hayashi Uji Shrine of Kawachi Province was recognized as a state shrine. Another entry from the 20th day of the 12th month in the 15th year of Jōgan (873) records the elevation of the enshrined deity Ame-no-Oshihi to the rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. During the {{ill|Sengoku period (Japan)|ja|戦国時代}}, the shrine was burned down by the forces of {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|ja|織田信長}}. After the extinction of the Tomobayashi clan, local residents reconstructed the shrine as a place to worship their {{ill|Ujigami|ja|産土神}}. In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the shrine's identification as Tomobayashi Uji Shrine was debated among {{ill|Kokugaku|lt=Kokugaku scholars|ja|国学}}. Despite inquiries by Edo officials, local village leaders at the time claimed it was a shrine dedicated to Gozu Tennō. By the late Edo period, the shrine became recognized as a site for worshiping the ancestral deity of the Ōtomo clan, influenced by the work of {{ill|Tomobayashi Mitsuhei|ja|伴林光平}}, a Kokugaku scholar from the village.Keita Endō, "Studies and Restoration of Shikinaisha: Investigation of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine" (First published in ''Shidai Nihonshi'', Vol. 5, 2002 / Collected in Endō, ''Formation of the Nihon Shoki and Related Materials'', Hanawa Shobō, 2015). In 1872 (Meiji 5), the shrine was designated as a village shrine. In 1932 (Shōwa 7), a survey commemorating the 50th anniversary of the {{ill|Imperial Rescript to Soldiers and Sailors|ja|軍人勅諭}} confirmed that this shrine is the only one in Japan dedicated to Michinoomi no Mikoto, the military deity of the Ōtomo clan. Consequently, it gained attention as the "Western Yasukuni Shrine." This designation heavily relied on the interpretation of Kokugaku scholar and historian {{ill|Ikuta Namekine|ja|生田目経徳}}. Subsequent renovations and expansions led to the completion of new shrine buildings in 1940 (Shōwa 15), including the relocation of a {{ill|Chōzuya|ja|手水舎}} donated by {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}} in Tokyo. The shrine was elevated to the rank of prefectural shrine in 1942 (Shōwa 17). The shrine marker was inscribed by {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}}. == Grounds == * Main Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Offering Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Corridor Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940. * Gate (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940. * Shrine Office * Chōzuya (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Donated by Yasukuni Shrine in 1940; originally constructed in 1872. === Sub-Shrines === * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Formerly the reliquary hall of Sawada Elementary School (now Dōmyōji Elementary School). == Cultural Properties == === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022.Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 100, June 29, 2022. * Worship Hall and Offering Hall of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Gate and Corridor Fence of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Chōzuya of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Hall (Former Sawada Elementary School Reliquary Hall) - Registered June 29, 2022. == location == * Address: Hayashi 3-chome 6-30, Fujidera City, Osaka Prefecture == Access == * By rail: 10-minute walk northwest (approximately 800m) from {{ill|Hajinonosato Station|ja|土師ノ里駅}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == External links == {{Commonscat|Tomobayashiuji-jinja}} *[http://www.tomobayashiuji-jinja.jp/index.html Tomobayashi Uji Shrine] [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Fujidera]] [[Category:History of Fujidera]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Former prefectural shrines in Osaka]] [[Category:bump]] [[Category:Tomobayashi Shrine]][[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:Tomobayashi Shrine]][[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]] [[ja:伴林氏神社]]

originally constructed in 1872. === Sub-Shrines === * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Formerly the reliquary hall of Sawada Elementary School (now Dōmyōji Elementary School). == Cultural Properties == === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022.Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 100, June 29, 2022. * Worship Hall and Offering Hall of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Gate and Corridor Fence of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Chōzuya of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Hall (Former Sawada Elementary School Reliquary Hall) - Registered June 29, 2022. == location == * Address: Hayashi 3-chome 6-30, Fujidera City, Osaka Prefecture == Access == * By rail: 10-minute walk northwest (approximately 800m) from {{ill|Hajinonosato Station|ja|土師ノ里駅}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == External links == {{Commonscat|Tomobayashiuji-jinja}} *[http://www.tomobayashiuji-jinja.jp/index.html Tomobayashi Uji Shrine] [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Fujidera]] [[Category:History of Fujidera]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Former prefectural shrines in Osaka]] [[Category:bump]] [[Category:Tomobayashi Shrine]][[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:Tomobayashi Shrine]][[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]] [[ja:伴林氏神社]]

revision.text:
{{Shrine | name = Tomobayashi Uji Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Tomobayashiuji-jinja, haiden.jpg|280px]]
Worship Hall | location = {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}{{ill|Fujidera City|ja|藤井寺市}} Hayashi 3-chome 6-30 | Latitude degrees = 34 | Latitude minutes = 34 | Latitude seconds = 32.649 | N/S = N | Longitude degrees = 135 | Longitude minutes = 36 | Longitude seconds = 40.439 | E/W = E | Map country code = JP | Map = Japan Osaka | Deities = {{ill|Takami-Musubi no Kami|ja|高皇産霊神}} | Shrine rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor)
Former {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|府社}} | consecration_year = Unknown | Main Hall = | Other names = | Pilgrimage sites = | festival = October 9 | Rituals = }} [[File:Tomobayashiuji-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Tomobayashi Uji Shrine''' (伴林氏神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Fujidera City|ja|藤井寺市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, in the Hayashi area of the former Haishi-go, Shiki District, Kawachi Province. The shrine is listed as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} and previously held the rank of {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|府社}}. It was once referred to as the "Western {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}}." == Deities == * Primary deity - {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|高皇産霊神}} (たかむすひのかみ) * Secondary deities - {{ill|Michinoomi no Mikoto|ja|道臣命}} (みちのおみのみこと), {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天押日命}} (あめのおしひのみこと) The shrine's name suggests that it was originally established by the {{ill|Tomobayashi clan|ja|伴林氏}}, a branch of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}, to worship their ancestral deities. Takami-Musubi no Kami is identified as the fifth-generation ancestor of Ame-no-Oshihi, who in turn is the third-generation ancestor of Michinoomi no Mikoto. == History == [[File:Tomobayashiuji-jinja, chouzusha.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Chōzuya}}]] The founding year of the shrine is unknown. According to the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}, an entry dated the 26th day of the 2nd month in the 9th year of Jōgan (867) mentions that the Hayashi Uji Shrine of Kawachi Province was recognized as a state shrine. Another entry from the 20th day of the 12th month in the 15th year of Jōgan (873) records the elevation of the enshrined deity Ame-no-Oshihi to the rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. During the {{ill|Sengoku period (Japan)|ja|戦国時代}}, the shrine was burned down by the forces of {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|ja|織田信長}}. After the extinction of the Tomobayashi clan, local residents reconstructed the shrine as a place to worship their {{ill|Ujigami|ja|産土神}}. In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the shrine's identification as Tomobayashi Uji Shrine was debated among {{ill|Kokugaku|lt=Kokugaku scholars|ja|国学}}. Despite inquiries by Edo officials, local village leaders at the time claimed it was a shrine dedicated to Gozu Tennō. By the late Edo period, the shrine became recognized as a site for worshiping the ancestral deity of the Ōtomo clan, influenced by the work of {{ill|Tomobayashi Mitsuhei|ja|伴林光平}}, a Kokugaku scholar from the village.Keita Endō, "Studies and Restoration of Shikinaisha: Investigation of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine" (First published in ''Shidai Nihonshi'', Vol. 5, 2002 / Collected in Endō, ''Formation of the Nihon Shoki and Related Materials'', Hanawa Shobō, 2015). In 1872 (Meiji 5), the shrine was designated as a village shrine. In 1932 (Shōwa 7), a survey commemorating the 50th anniversary of the {{ill|Imperial Rescript to Soldiers and Sailors|ja|軍人勅諭}} confirmed that this shrine is the only one in Japan dedicated to Michinoomi no Mikoto, the military deity of the Ōtomo clan. Consequently, it gained attention as the "Western Yasukuni Shrine." This designation heavily relied on the interpretation of Kokugaku scholar and historian {{ill|Ikuta namekine|ja|生田目経徳}}. Subsequent renovations and expansions led to the completion of new shrine buildings in 1940 (Shōwa 15), including the relocation of a {{ill|Chōzuya|ja|手水舎}} donated by {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}} in Tokyo. The shrine was elevated to the rank of prefectural shrine in 1942 (Shōwa 17). The shrine marker was inscribed by {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}}. == Grounds == * Main Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Offering Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Corridor Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940. * Gate (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940. * Shrine Office * Chōzuya (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Donated by Yasukuni Shrine in 1940; originally constructed in 1872. === Sub-Shrines === * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Formerly the reliquary hall of Sawada Elementary School (now Dōmyōji Elementary School). == Cultural Properties == === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022.Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 100, June 29, 2022. * Worship Hall and Offering Hall of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Gate and Corridor Fence of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Chōzuya of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Hall (Former Sawada Elementary School Reliquary Hall) - Registered June 29, 2022. == location == * Address: Hayashi 3-chome 6-30, Fujidera City, Osaka Prefecture == Access == * By rail: 10-minute walk northwest (approximately 800m) from {{ill|Hajinonosato Station|ja|土師ノ里駅}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == External links == {{Commonscat|Tomobayashiuji-jinja}} *[http://www.tomobayashiuji-jinja.jp/index.html Tomobayashi Uji Shrine] [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Fujidera]] [[Category:History of Fujidera]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Former prefectural shrines in Osaka]] [[Category:bump]] [[Category:Tomobayashi Shrine]][[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:Tomobayashi Shrine]][[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]] [[ja:伴林氏神社]]

By the late Edo period, the shrine became recognized as a site for worshiping the ancestral deity of the Ōtomo clan, influenced by the work of {{ill|Tomobayashi Mitsuhei|ja|伴林光平}}, a Kokugaku scholar from the village.Keita Endō, "Studies and Restoration of Shikinaisha: Investigation of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine" (First published in ''Shidai Nihonshi'', Vol. 5, 2002 / Collected in Endō, ''Formation of the Nihon Shoki and Related Materials'', Hanawa Shobō, 2015). In 1872 (Meiji 5), the shrine was designated as a village shrine. In 1932 (Shōwa 7), a survey commemorating the 50th anniversary of the {{ill|Imperial Rescript to Soldiers and Sailors|ja|軍人勅諭}} confirmed that this shrine is the only one in Japan dedicated to Michinoomi no Mikoto, the military deity of the Ōtomo clan. Consequently, it gained attention as the "Western Yasukuni Shrine." This designation heavily relied on the interpretation of Kokugaku scholar and historian {{ill|Ikuta namekine|ja|生田目経徳}}. Subsequent renovations and expansions led to the completion of new shrine buildings in 1940 (Shōwa 15), including the relocation of a {{ill|Chōzuya|ja|手水舎}} donated by {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}} in Tokyo. The shrine was elevated to the rank of prefectural shrine in 1942 (Shōwa 17). The shrine marker was inscribed by {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}}. == Grounds == * Main Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Offering Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Corridor Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940. * Gate (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940. * Shrine Office * Chōzuya (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Donated by Yasukuni Shrine in 1940; originally constructed in 1872. === Sub-Shrines === * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Formerly the reliquary hall of Sawada Elementary School (now Dōmyōji Elementary School). == Cultural Properties == === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022.Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 100, June 29, 2022. * Worship Hall and Offering Hall of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Gate and Corridor Fence of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Chōzuya of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Hall (Former Sawada Elementary School Reliquary Hall) - Registered June 29, 2022. == location == * Address: Hayashi 3-chome 6-30, Fujidera City, Osaka Prefecture == Access == * By rail: 10-minute walk northwest (approximately 800m) from {{ill|Hajinonosato Station|ja|土師ノ里駅}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == External links == {{Commonscat|Tomobayashiuji-jinja}} *[http://www.tomobayashiuji-jinja.jp/index.html Tomobayashi Uji Shrine] [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Fujidera]] [[Category:History of Fujidera]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Former prefectural shrines in Osaka]] [[Category:bump]] [[Category:Tomobayashi Shrine]][[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:Tomobayashi Shrine]][[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Tomobayashi Shrine]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]] [[ja:伴林氏神社]]

{{Shrine | name = Tomobayashi Uji Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Tomobayashiuji-jinja, haiden.jpg|280px]]
Worship Hall | location = {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}{{ill|Fujidera City|ja|藤井寺市}} Hayashi 3-chome 6-30 | Latitude degrees = 34 | Latitude minutes = 34 | Latitude seconds = 32.649 | N/S = N | Longitude degrees = 135 | Longitude minutes = 36 | Longitude seconds = 40.439 | E/W = E | Map country code = JP | Map = Japan Osaka | Deities = {{ill|Takami-Musubi no Kami|ja|高皇産霊神}} | Shrine rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor)
Former {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|府社}} | consecration_year = Unknown | Main Hall = | Other names = | Pilgrimage sites = | festival = October 9 | Rituals = }} [[File:Tomobayashiuji-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Tomobayashi Uji Shrine''' (伴林氏神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Fujidera City|ja|藤井寺市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, in the Hayashi area of the former Haishi-go, Shiki District, Kawachi Province. The shrine is listed as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} and previously held the rank of {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|府社}}. It was once referred to as the "Western {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}}." == Deities == * Primary deity - {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|高皇産霊神}} (たかむすひのかみ) * Secondary deities - {{ill|Michinoomi no Mikoto|ja|道臣命}} (みちのおみのみこと), {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天押日命}} (あめのおしひのみこと) The shrine's name suggests that it was originally established by the {{ill|Tomobayashi clan|ja|伴林氏}}, a branch of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}, to worship their ancestral deities. Takami-Musubi no Kami is identified as the fifth-generation ancestor of Ame-no-Oshihi, who in turn is the third-generation ancestor of Michinoomi no Mikoto. == History == [[File:Tomobayashiuji-jinja, chouzusha.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Chōzuya}}]] The founding year of the shrine is unknown. According to the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}, an entry dated the 26th day of the 2nd month in the 9th year of Jōgan (867) mentions that the Hayashi Uji Shrine of Kawachi Province was recognized as a state shrine. Another entry from the 20th day of the 12th month in the 15th year of Jōgan (873) records the elevation of the enshrined deity Ame-no-Oshihi to the rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. During the {{ill|Sengoku period (Japan)|ja|戦国時代}}, the shrine was burned down by the forces of {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|ja|織田信長}}. After the extinction of the Tomobayashi clan, local residents reconstructed the shrine as a place to worship their {{ill|Ujigami|ja|産土神}}. In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the shrine's identification as Tomobayashi Uji Shrine was debated among {{ill|Kokugaku|lt=Kokugaku scholars|ja|国学}}. Despite inquiries by Edo officials, local village leaders at the time claimed it was a shrine dedicated to Gozu Tennō. By the late Edo period, the shrine became recognized as a site for worshiping the ancestral deity of the Ōtomo clan, influenced by the work of {{ill|Tomobayashi Mitsuhei|ja|伴林光平}}, a Kokugaku scholar from the village.Keita Endō, "Studies and Restoration of Shikinaisha: Investigation of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine" (First published in ''Shidai Nihonshi'', Vol. 5, 2002 / Collected in Endō, ''Formation of the Nihon Shoki and Related Materials'', Hanawa Shobō, 2015). In 1872 (Meiji 5), the shrine was designated as a village shrine. In 1932 (Shōwa 7), a survey commemorating the 50th anniversary of the {{ill|Imperial Rescript to Soldiers and Sailors|ja|軍人勅諭}} confirmed that this shrine is the only one in Japan dedicated to Michinoomi no Mikoto, the military deity of the Ōtomo clan. Consequently, it gained attention as the "Western Yasukuni Shrine." This designation heavily relied on the interpretation of Kokugaku scholar and historian {{ill|Ikuta namekine|ja|生田目経徳}}. Subsequent renovations and expansions led to the completion of new shrine buildings in 1940 (Shōwa 15), including the relocation of a {{ill|Chōzuya|ja|手水舎}} donated by {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}} in Tokyo. The shrine was elevated to the rank of prefectural shrine in 1942 (Shōwa 17). The shrine marker was inscribed by {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}}. == Grounds == * Main Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Offering Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Corridor Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940. * Gate (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940. * Shrine Office * Chōzuya (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Donated by Yasukuni Shrine in 1940; originally constructed in 1872. === Sub-Shrines === * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Formerly the reliquary hall of Sawada Elementary School (now Dōmyōji Elementary School). == Cultural Properties == === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022.Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 100, June 29, 2022. * Worship Hall and Offering Hall of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Gate and Corridor Fence of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Chōzuya of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Hall (Former Sawada Elementary School Reliquary Hall) - Registered June 29, 2022. == location == * Address: Hayashi 3-chome 6-30, Fujidera City, Osaka Prefecture == Access == * By rail: 10-minute walk northwest (approximately 800m) from {{ill|Hajinonosato Station|ja|土師ノ里駅}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == External links == {{Commonscat|Tomobayashiuji-jinja}} *[http://www.tomobayashiuji-jinja.jp/index.html Tomobayashi Uji Shrine] [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Fujidera]] [[Category:History of Fujidera]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Former prefectural shrines in Osaka]] [[Category:bump]] [[Category:Tomobayashi Shrine]][[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:Tomobayashi Shrine]][[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Tomobayashi Shrine]][[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]] [[ja:伴林氏神社]]

It was once referred to as the "Western {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}}." == Deities == * Primary deity - {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|高皇産霊神}} (たかむすひのかみ) * Secondary deities - {{ill|Michinoomi no Mikoto|ja|道臣命}} (みちのおみのみこと), {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天押日命}} (あめのおしひのみこと) The shrine's name suggests that it was originally established by the {{ill|Tomobayashi clan|ja|伴林氏}}, a branch of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}, to worship their ancestral deities. Takami-Musubi no Kami is identified as the fifth-generation ancestor of Ame-no-Oshihi, who in turn is the third-generation ancestor of Michinoomi no Mikoto. == History == [[File:Tomobayashiuji-jinja, chouzusha.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Chōzuya}}]] The founding year of the shrine is unknown. According to the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}, an entry dated the 26th day of the 2nd month in the 9th year of Jōgan (867) mentions that the Hayashi Uji Shrine of Kawachi Province was recognized as a state shrine. Another entry from the 20th day of the 12th month in the 15th year of Jōgan (873) records the elevation of the enshrined deity Ame-no-Oshihi to the rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. During the {{ill|Sengoku period (Japan)|ja|戦国時代}}, the shrine was burned down by the forces of {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|ja|織田信長}}. After the extinction of the Tomobayashi clan, local residents reconstructed the shrine as a place to worship their {{ill|Ujigami|ja|産土神}}. In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the shrine's identification as Tomobayashi Uji Shrine was debated among {{ill|Kokugaku|lt=Kokugaku scholars|ja|国学}}. Despite inquiries by Edo officials, local village leaders at the time claimed it was a shrine dedicated to Gozu Tennō. By the late Edo period, the shrine became recognized as a site for worshiping the ancestral deity of the Ōtomo clan, influenced by the work of {{ill|Tomobayashi Mitsuhei|ja|伴林光平}}, a Kokugaku scholar from the village.Keita Endō, "Studies and Restoration of Shikinaisha: Investigation of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine" (First published in ''Shidai Nihonshi'', Vol. 5, 2002 / Collected in Endō, ''Formation of the Nihon Shoki and Related Materials'', Hanawa Shobō, 2015). In 1872 (Meiji 5), the shrine was designated as a village shrine. In 1932 (Shōwa 7), a survey commemorating the 50th anniversary of the {{ill|Imperial Rescript to Soldiers and Sailors|ja|軍人勅諭}} confirmed that this shrine is the only one in Japan dedicated to Michinoomi no Mikoto, the military deity of the Ōtomo clan. Consequently, it gained attention as the "Western Yasukuni Shrine." This designation heavily relied on the interpretation of Kokugaku scholar and historian {{ill|Ikuta namekine|ja|生田目経徳}}. Subsequent renovations and expansions led to the completion of new shrine buildings in 1940 (Shōwa 15), including the relocation of a {{ill|Chōzuya|ja|手水舎}} donated by {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}} in Tokyo. The shrine was elevated to the rank of prefectural shrine in 1942 (Shōwa 17). The shrine marker was inscribed by {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}}. == Grounds == * Main Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Offering Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Corridor Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940. * Gate (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940. * Shrine Office * Chōzuya (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Donated by Yasukuni Shrine in 1940; originally constructed in 1872. === Sub-Shrines === * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Formerly the reliquary hall of Sawada Elementary School (now Dōmyōji Elementary School). == Cultural Properties == === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022.Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 100, June 29, 2022. * Worship Hall and Offering Hall of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Gate and Corridor Fence of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Chōzuya of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Hall (Former Sawada Elementary School Reliquary Hall) - Registered June 29, 2022. == location == * Address: Hayashi 3-chome 6-30, Fujidera City, Osaka Prefecture == Access == * By rail: 10-minute walk northwest (approximately 800m) from {{ill|Hajinonosato Station|ja|土師ノ里駅}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == External links == {{Commonscat|Tomobayashiuji-jinja}} *[http://www.tomobayashiuji-jinja.jp/index.html Tomobayashi Uji Shrine] [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Fujidera]] [[Category:History of Fujidera]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Former prefectural shrines in Osaka]] [[Category:bump]] [[Category:Tomobayashi Shrine]][[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:Tomobayashi Shrine]][[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Tomobayashi Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Tomobayashi Shrine]][[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]] [[ja:伴林氏神社]]

originally constructed in 1872. === Sub-Shrines === * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Formerly the reliquary hall of Sawada Elementary School (now Dōmyōji Elementary School). == Cultural Properties == === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022.Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 100, June 29, 2022. * Worship Hall and Offering Hall of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Gate and Corridor Fence of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Chōzuya of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Hall (Former Sawada Elementary School Reliquary Hall) - Registered June 29, 2022. == location == * Address: Hayashi 3-chome 6-30, Fujidera City, Osaka Prefecture == Access == * By rail: 10-minute walk northwest (approximately 800m) from {{ill|Hajinonosato Station|ja|土師ノ里駅}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == External links == {{Commonscat|Tomobayashiuji-jinja}} *[http://www.tomobayashiuji-jinja.jp/index.html Tomobayashi Uji Shrine] [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Fujidera]] [[Category:History of Fujidera]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Former prefectural shrines in Osaka]] [[Category:bump]] [[Category:Tomobayashi Shrine]][[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:Tomobayashi Shrine]][[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Tomobayashi Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Tomobayashi Shrine]][[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:Subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:qq]] [[ja:伴林氏神社]]

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Tomobayashi Uji Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Tomobayashiuji-jinja, haiden.jpg|280px]]
Worship Hall | location = {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}{{ill|Fujidera City|ja|藤井寺市}} Hayashi 3-chome 6-30 | Latitude degrees = 34 | Latitude minutes = 34 | Latitude seconds = 32.649 | N/S = N | Longitude degrees = 135 | Longitude minutes = 36 | Longitude seconds = 40.439 | E/W = E | Map country code = JP | Map = Japan Osaka | Deities = {{ill|Takami-Musubi no Kami|ja|高皇産霊神}} | Shrine rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor)
Former {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|府社}} | consecration_year = Unknown | Main Hall = | Other names = | Pilgrimage sites = | festival = October 9 | Rituals = }} [[File:Tomobayashiuji-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Tomobayashi Uji Shrine''' (伴林氏神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Fujidera City|ja|藤井寺市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, in the Hayashi area of the former Haishi-go, Shiki District, Kawachi Province. The shrine is listed as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} and previously held the rank of {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|府社}}. It was once referred to as the "Western {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}}." == Deities == * Primary deity - {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|高皇産霊神}} (たかむすひのかみ) * Secondary deities - {{ill|Michinoomi no Mikoto|ja|道臣命}} (みちのおみのみこと), {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天押日命}} (あめのおしひのみこと) The shrine's name suggests that it was originally established by the {{ill|Tomobayashi clan|ja|伴林氏}}, a branch of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}, to worship their ancestral deities. Takami-Musubi no Kami is identified as the fifth-generation ancestor of Ame-no-Oshihi, who in turn is the third-generation ancestor of Michinoomi no Mikoto. == History == [[File:Tomobayashiuji-jinja, chouzusha.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Chōzuya}}]] The founding year of the shrine is unknown. According to the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}, an entry dated the 26th day of the 2nd month in the 9th year of Jōgan (867) mentions that the Hayashi Uji Shrine of Kawachi Province was recognized as a state shrine. Another entry from the 20th day of the 12th month in the 15th year of Jōgan (873) records the elevation of the enshrined deity Ame-no-Oshihi to the rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. During the {{ill|Sengoku period (Japan)|ja|戦国時代}}, the shrine was burned down by the forces of {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|ja|織田信長}}. After the extinction of the Tomobayashi clan, local residents reconstructed the shrine as a place to worship their {{ill|Ujigami|ja|産土神}}. In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the shrine's identification as Tomobayashi Uji Shrine was debated among {{ill|Kokugaku|lt=Kokugaku scholars|ja|国学}}. Despite inquiries by Edo officials, local village leaders at the time claimed it was a shrine dedicated to Gozu Tennō. By the late Edo period, the shrine became recognized as a site for worshiping the ancestral deity of the Ōtomo clan, influenced by the work of {{ill|Tomobayashi Mitsuhei|ja|伴林光平}}, a Kokugaku scholar from the village.Keita Endō, "Studies and Restoration of Shikinaisha: Investigation of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine" (First published in ''Shidai Nihonshi'', Vol. 5, 2002 / Collected in Endō, ''Formation of the Nihon Shoki and Related Materials'', Hanawa Shobō, 2015). In 1872 (Meiji 5), the shrine was designated as a village shrine. In 1932 (Shōwa 7), a survey commemorating the 50th anniversary of the {{ill|Imperial Rescript to Soldiers and Sailors|ja|軍人勅諭}} confirmed that this shrine is the only one in Japan dedicated to Michinoomi no Mikoto, the military deity of the Ōtomo clan. Consequently, it gained attention as the "Western Yasukuni Shrine." This designation heavily relied on the interpretation of Kokugaku scholar and historian {{ill|Ikuta namekine|ja|生田目経徳}}. Subsequent renovations and expansions led to the completion of new shrine buildings in 1940 (Shōwa 15), including the relocation of a {{ill|Chōzuya|ja|手水舎}} donated by {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}} in Tokyo. The shrine was elevated to the rank of prefectural shrine in 1942 (Shōwa 17). The shrine marker was inscribed by {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}}. == Grounds == * Main Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Offering Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Corridor Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940. * Gate (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940. * Shrine Office * Chōzuya (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Donated by Yasukuni Shrine in 1940; originally constructed in 1872. === Sub-Shrines === * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Formerly the reliquary hall of Sawada Elementary School (now Dōmyōji Elementary School). == Cultural Properties == === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022.Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 100, June 29, 2022. * Worship Hall and Offering Hall of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Gate and Corridor Fence of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Chōzuya of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Hall (Former Sawada Elementary School Reliquary Hall) - Registered June 29, 2022. == location == * Address: Hayashi 3-chome 6-30, Fujidera City, Osaka Prefecture == Access == * By rail: 10-minute walk northwest (approximately 800m) from {{ill|Hajinonosato Station|ja|土師ノ里駅}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == External links == {{Commonscat|Tomobayashiuji-jinja}} *[http://www.tomobayashiuji-jinja.jp/index.html Tomobayashi Uji Shrine] [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Fujidera]] [[Category:History of Fujidera]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Former prefectural shrines in Osaka]] [[Category:bump]] [[Category:Tomobayashi Shrine]][[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:Tomobayashi Shrine]][[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Tomobayashi Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Tomobayashi Shrine]][[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:Subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:qq]] [[ja:伴林氏神社]]

Takami-Musubi no Kami is identified as the fifth-generation ancestor of Ame-no-Oshihi, who in turn is the third-generation ancestor of Michinoomi no Mikoto. == History == [[File:Tomobayashiuji-jinja, chouzusha.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Chōzuya}}]] The founding year of the shrine is unknown. According to the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}, an entry dated the 26th day of the 2nd month in the 9th year of Jōgan (867) mentions that the Hayashi Uji Shrine of Kawachi Province was recognized as a state shrine. Another entry from the 20th day of the 12th month in the 15th year of Jōgan (873) records the elevation of the enshrined deity Ame-no-Oshihi to the rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. During the {{ill|Sengoku period (Japan)|ja|戦国時代}}, the shrine was burned down by the forces of {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|ja|織田信長}}. After the extinction of the Tomobayashi clan, local residents reconstructed the shrine as a place to worship their {{ill|Ujigami|ja|産土神}}. In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the shrine's identification as Tomobayashi Uji Shrine was debated among {{ill|Kokugaku|lt=Kokugaku scholars|ja|国学}}. Despite inquiries by Edo officials, local village leaders at the time claimed it was a shrine dedicated to Gozu Tennō. By the late Edo period, the shrine became recognized as a site for worshiping the ancestral deity of the Ōtomo clan, influenced by the work of {{ill|Tomobayashi Mitsuhei|ja|伴林光平}}, a Kokugaku scholar from the village.Keita Endō, "Studies and Restoration of Shikinaisha: Investigation of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine" (First published in ''Shidai Nihonshi'', Vol. 5, 2002 / Collected in Endō, ''Formation of the Nihon Shoki and Related Materials'', Hanawa Shobō, 2015). In 1872 (Meiji 5), the shrine was designated as a village shrine. In 1932 (Shōwa 7), a survey commemorating the 50th anniversary of the {{ill|Imperial Rescript to Soldiers and Sailors|ja|軍人勅諭}} confirmed that this shrine is the only one in Japan dedicated to Michinoomi no Mikoto, the military deity of the Ōtomo clan. Consequently, it gained attention as the "Western Yasukuni Shrine." This designation heavily relied on the interpretation of Kokugaku scholar and historian {{ill|Ikuta namekine|ja|生田目経徳}}. Subsequent renovations and expansions led to the completion of new shrine buildings in 1940 (Shōwa 15), including the relocation of a {{ill|Chōzuya|ja|手水舎}} donated by {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}} in Tokyo. The shrine was elevated to the rank of prefectural shrine in 1942 (Shōwa 17). The shrine marker was inscribed by {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}}. == Grounds == * Main Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Offering Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Corridor Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940. * Gate (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940. * Shrine Office * Chōzuya (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Donated by Yasukuni Shrine in 1940; originally constructed in 1872. === Sub-Shrines === * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Formerly the reliquary hall of Sawada Elementary School (now Dōmyōji Elementary School). == Cultural Properties == === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022.Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 100, June 29, 2022. * Worship Hall and Offering Hall of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Gate and Corridor Fence of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Chōzuya of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Hall (Former Sawada Elementary School Reliquary Hall) - Registered June 29, 2022. == location == * Address: Hayashi 3-chome 6-30, Fujidera City, Osaka Prefecture == Access == * By rail: 10-minute walk northwest (approximately 800m) from {{ill|Hajinonosato Station|ja|土師ノ里駅}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == External links == {{Commonscat|Tomobayashiuji-jinja}} *[http://www.tomobayashiuji-jinja.jp/index.html Tomobayashi Uji Shrine] [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Fujidera]] [[Category:History of Fujidera]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Former prefectural shrines in Osaka]] [[Category:bump]] [[Category:Tomobayashi Shrine]][[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:Tomobayashi Shrine]][[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Tomobayashi Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Tomobayashi Shrine]][[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:Subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]][[sn:Tomobayashi Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tomobayashi Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tomobayashi Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tomobayashi Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tomobayashi Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]][[sn:Tomobayashi Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tomobayashi Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tomobayashi Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tomobayashi Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tomobayashi Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]][[sn:Tomobayashi Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tomobayashi Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tomobayashi Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tomobayashi Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tomobayashi Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:伴林氏神社]]

By the late Edo period, the shrine became recognized as a site for worshiping the ancestral deity of the Ōtomo clan, influenced by the work of {{ill|Tomobayashi Mitsuhei|ja|伴林光平}}, a Kokugaku scholar from the village.Keita Endō, "Studies and Restoration of Shikinaisha: Investigation of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine" (First published in ''Shidai Nihonshi'', Vol. 5, 2002 / Collected in Endō, ''Formation of the Nihon Shoki and Related Materials'', Hanawa Shobō, 2015). In 1872 (Meiji 5), the shrine was designated as a village shrine. In 1932 (Shōwa 7), a survey commemorating the 50th anniversary of the {{ill|Imperial Rescript to Soldiers and Sailors|ja|軍人勅諭}} confirmed that this shrine is the only one in Japan dedicated to Michinoomi no Mikoto, the military deity of the Ōtomo clan. Consequently, it gained attention as the "Western Yasukuni Shrine." This designation heavily relied on the interpretation of Kokugaku scholar and historian {{ill|Ikuta namekine|ja|生田目経徳}}. Subsequent renovations and expansions led to the completion of new shrine buildings in 1940 (Shōwa 15), including the relocation of a {{ill|Chōzuya|ja|手水舎}} donated by {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}} in Tokyo. The shrine was elevated to the rank of prefectural shrine in 1942 (Shōwa 17). The shrine marker was inscribed by {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}}. == Grounds == * Main Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Offering Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Corridor Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940. * Gate (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940. * Shrine Office * Chōzuya (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Donated by Yasukuni Shrine in 1940; originally constructed in 1872. === Sub-Shrines === * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Formerly the reliquary hall of Sawada Elementary School (now Dōmyōji Elementary School). == Cultural Properties == === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022.Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 100, June 29, 2022. * Worship Hall and Offering Hall of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Gate and Corridor Fence of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Chōzuya of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Hall (Former Sawada Elementary School Reliquary Hall) - Registered June 29, 2022. == location == * Address: Hayashi 3-chome 6-30, Fujidera City, Worship Hall | location = {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}{{ill|Fujidera City|ja|藤井寺市}} Hayashi 3-chome 6-30 | Latitude degrees = 34 | Latitude minutes = 34 | Latitude seconds = 32.649 | N/S = N | Longitude degrees = 135 | Longitude minutes = 36 | Longitude seconds = 40.439 | E/W = E | Map country code = JP | Map = Japan Osaka | Deities = {{ill|Takami-Musubi no Kami|ja|高皇産霊神}} | Shrine rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor)
Former {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|府社}} | consecration_year = Unknown | Main Hall = | Other names = | Pilgrimage sites = | festival = October 9 | Rituals = }} [[File:Tomobayashiuji-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Tomobayashi Uji Shrine''' (伴林氏神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Fujidera City|ja|藤井寺市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, in the Hayashi area of the former Haishi-go, Shiki District, Kawachi Province. The shrine is listed as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} and previously held the rank of {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|府社}}. It was once referred to as the "Western {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}}." == Deities == * Primary deity - {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|高皇産霊神}} (たかむすひのかみ) * Secondary deities - {{ill|Michinoomi no Mikoto|ja|道臣命}} (みちのおみのみこと), {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天押日命}} (あめのおしひのみこと) The shrine's name suggests that it was originally established by the {{ill|Tomobayashi clan|ja|伴林氏}}, a branch of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}, to worship their ancestral deities. Takami-Musubi no Kami is identified as the fifth-generation ancestor of Ame-no-Oshihi, who in turn is the third-generation ancestor of Michinoomi no Mikoto. == History == [[File:Tomobayashiuji-jinja, chouzusha.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Chōzuya}}]] The founding year of the shrine is unknown. According to the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}, an entry dated the 26th day of the 2nd month in the 9th year of Jōgan (867) mentions that the Hayashi Uji Shrine of Kawachi Province was recognized as a state shrine. Another entry from the 20th day of the 12th month in the 15th year of Jōgan (873) records the elevation of the enshrined deity Ame-no-Oshihi to the rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. During the {{ill|Sengoku period (Japan)|ja|戦国時代}}, the shrine was burned down by the forces of {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|ja|織田信長}}. After the extinction of the Tomobayashi clan, local residents reconstructed the shrine as a place to worship their {{ill|Ujigami|ja|産土神}}. In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the shrine's identification as Tomobayashi Uji Shrine was debated among {{ill|Kokugaku|lt=Kokugaku scholars|ja|国学}}. Despite inquiries by Edo officials, local village leaders at the time claimed it was a shrine dedicated to Gozu Tennō. By the late Edo period, the shrine became recognized as a site for worshiping the ancestral deity of the Ōtomo clan, influenced by the work of {{ill|Tomobayashi Mitsuhei|ja|伴林光平}}, a Kokugaku scholar from the village.Keita Endō, "Studies and Restoration of Shikinaisha: Investigation of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine" (First published in ''Shidai Nihonshi'', Vol. 5, 2002 / Collected in Endō, ''Formation of the Nihon Shoki and Related Materials'', Hanawa Shobō, 2015). In 1872 (Meiji 5), the shrine was designated as a village shrine. In 1932 (Shōwa 7), a survey commemorating the 50th anniversary of the {{ill|Imperial Rescript to Soldiers and Sailors|ja|軍人勅諭}} confirmed that this shrine is the only one in Japan dedicated to Michinoomi no Mikoto, the military deity of the Ōtomo clan. Consequently, it gained attention as the "Western Yasukuni Shrine." This designation heavily relied on the interpretation of Kokugaku scholar and historian {{ill|Ikuta namekine|ja|生田目経徳}}. Subsequent renovations and expansions led to the completion of new shrine buildings in 1940 (Shōwa 15), including the relocation of a {{ill|Chōzuya|ja|手水舎}} donated by {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}} in Tokyo. The shrine was elevated to the rank of prefectural shrine in 1942 (Shōwa 17). The shrine marker was inscribed by {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}}. == Grounds == * Main Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Offering Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Corridor Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940. * Gate (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940. * Shrine Office * Chōzuya (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Donated by Yasukuni Shrine in 1940; originally constructed in 1872. === Sub-Shrines === * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Formerly the reliquary hall of Sawada Elementary School (now Dōmyōji Elementary School). == Cultural Properties == === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022.Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 100, June 29, 2022. * - Registered June 29, 2022. * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Hall (Former Sawada Elementary School Reliquary Hall) - Registered June 29, 2022. == location == * Address: Hayashi 3-chome 6-30, Fujidera City, The shrine was elevated to the rank of prefectural shrine in 1942 (Shōwa 17). The shrine marker was inscribed by {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}}. == Grounds == * Main Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Offering Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Corridor Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940. * Gate (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940. * Shrine Office * Chōzuya (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Donated by Yasukuni Shrine in 1940; originally constructed in 1872. === Sub-Shrines === * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Formerly the reliquary hall of Sawada Elementary School (now Dōmyōji Elementary School). == Cultural Properties == === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022.Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 100, June 29, 2022. * Worship Hall and Offering Hall of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Gate and Corridor Fence of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Chōzuya of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Hall (Former Sawada Elementary School Reliquary Hall) - Registered June 29, 2022. == location == * Address: Hayashi 3-chome 6-30, Fujidera City, Osaka Prefecture == Access == * By rail: 10-minute walk northwest (approximately 800m) from {{ill|Hajinonosato Station|ja|土師ノ里駅}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線}}. == References == {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Tomobayashi Uji Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Tomobayashiuji-jinja, haiden.jpg|280px]]
Worship Hall | location = {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}{{ill|Fujidera City|ja|藤井寺市}} Hayashi 3-chome 6-30 | Latitude degrees = 34 | Latitude minutes = 34 | Latitude seconds = 32.649 | N/S = N | Longitude degrees = 135 | Longitude minutes = 36 | Longitude seconds = 40.439 | E/W = E | Map country code = JP | Map = Japan Osaka | Deities = {{ill|Takami-Musubi no Kami|ja|高皇産霊神}} | Shrine rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor)
Former {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|府社}} | consecration_year = Unknown | Main Hall = | Other names = | Pilgrimage sites = | festival = October 9 | Rituals = }} [[File:Tomobayashiuji-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Tomobayashi Uji Shrine''' (伴林氏神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Fujidera City|ja|藤井寺市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, in the Hayashi area of the former Haishi-go, Shiki District, Kawachi Province. The shrine is listed as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} and previously held the rank of {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|府社}}. It was once referred to as the "Western {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}}." == Deities == * Primary deity - {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|高皇産霊神}} (たかむすひのかみ) * Secondary deities - {{ill|Michinoomi no Mikoto|ja|道臣命}} (みちのおみのみこと), {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天押日命}} (あめのおしひのみこと) The shrine's name suggests that it was originally established by the {{ill|Tomobayashi clan|ja|伴林氏}}, a branch of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}, to worship their ancestral deities. Takami-Musubi no Kami is identified as the fifth-generation ancestor of Ame-no-Oshihi, who in turn is the third-generation ancestor of Michinoomi no Mikoto. == History == [[File:Tomobayashiuji-jinja, chouzusha.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Chōzuya}}]] The founding year of the shrine is unknown. According to the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}, an entry dated the 26th day of the 2nd month in the 9th year of Jōgan (867) mentions that the Hayashi Uji Shrine of Kawachi Province was recognized as a state shrine. Another entry from the 20th day of the 12th month in the 15th year of Jōgan (873) records the elevation of the enshrined deity Ame-no-Oshihi to the rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. During the {{ill|Sengoku period (Japan)|ja|戦国時代}}, the shrine was burned down by the forces of {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|ja|織田信長}}. After the extinction of the Tomobayashi clan, local residents reconstructed the shrine as a place to worship their {{ill|Ujigami|ja|産土神}}. In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the shrine's identification as Tomobayashi Uji Shrine was debated among {{ill|Kokugaku|lt=Kokugaku scholars|ja|国学}}. Despite inquiries by Edo officials, local village leaders at the time claimed it was a shrine dedicated to Gozu Tennō. By the late Edo period, the shrine became recognized as a site for worshiping the ancestral deity of the Ōtomo clan, influenced by the work of {{ill|Tomobayashi Mitsuhei|ja|伴林光平}}, a Kokugaku scholar from the village.Keita Endō, "Studies and Restoration of Shikinaisha: Investigation of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine" (First published in ''Shidai Nihonshi'', Vol. 5, 2002 / Collected in Endō, ''Formation of the Nihon Shoki and Related Materials'', Hanawa Shobō, 2015). In 1872 (Meiji 5), the shrine was designated as a village shrine. In 1932 (Shōwa 7), a survey commemorating the 50th anniversary of the {{ill|Imperial Rescript to Soldiers and Sailors|ja|軍人勅諭}} confirmed that this shrine is the only one in Japan dedicated to Michinoomi no Mikoto, the military deity of the Ōtomo clan. Consequently, it gained attention as the "Western Yasukuni Shrine." This designation heavily relied on the interpretation of Kokugaku scholar and historian {{ill|Ikuta namekine|ja|生田目経徳}}. Subsequent renovations and expansions led to the completion of new shrine buildings in 1940 (Shōwa 15), including the relocation of a {{ill|Chōzuya|ja|手水舎}} donated by {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}} in Tokyo. The shrine was elevated to the rank of prefectural shrine in 1942 (Shōwa 17). The shrine marker was inscribed by {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}}. == Grounds == * Main Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Offering Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Corridor Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940. * Gate (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940. * Shrine Office * Chōzuya (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Donated by Yasukuni Shrine in 1940; originally constructed in 1872. === Sub-Shrines === * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Formerly the reliquary hall of Sawada Elementary School (now Dōmyōji Elementary School). == Cultural Properties == === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022.Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 100, June 29, 2022. * Worship Hall and Offering Hall of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Gate and Corridor Fence of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Chōzuya of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Hall (Former Sawada Elementary School Reliquary Hall) - Registered June 29, 2022. == location == * Address: Hayashi 3-chome 6-30, Fujidera City, Osaka Prefecture == Access == * By rail: 10-minute walk northwest (approximately 800m) from {{ill|Hajinonosato Station|ja|土師ノ里駅}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線}}. == References == {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Tomobayashi Uji Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Tomobayashiuji-jinja, haiden.jpg|280px]]
Worship Hall | location = {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}{{ill|Fujidera City|ja|藤井寺市}} Hayashi 3-chome 6-30 | Latitude degrees = 34 | Latitude minutes = 34 | Latitude seconds = 32.649 | N/S = N | Longitude degrees = 135 | Longitude minutes = 36 | Longitude seconds = 40.439 | E/W = E | Map country code = JP | Map = Japan Osaka | Deities = {{ill|Takami-Musubi no Kami|ja|高皇産霊神}} | Shrine rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor)
Former {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|府社}} | consecration_year = Unknown | Main Hall = | Other names = | Pilgrimage sites = | festival = October 9 | Rituals = }} [[File:Tomobayashiuji-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Tomobayashi Uji Shrine''' (伴林氏神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Fujidera City|ja|藤井寺市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, in the Hayashi area of the former Haishi-go, Shiki District, Kawachi Province. The shrine is listed as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} and previously held the rank of {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|府社}}. It was once referred to as the "Western {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}}." == Deities == * Primary deity - {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|高皇産霊神}} (たかむすひのかみ) * Secondary deities - {{ill|Michinoomi no Mikoto|ja|道臣命}} (みちのおみのみこと), {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天押日命}} (あめのおしひのみこと) The shrine's name suggests that it was originally established by the {{ill|Tomobayashi clan|ja|伴林氏}}, a branch of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}, to worship their ancestral deities. Takami-Musubi no Kami is identified as the fifth-generation ancestor of Ame-no-Oshihi, who in turn is the third-generation ancestor of Michinoomi no Mikoto. == History == [[File:Tomobayashiuji-jinja, chouzusha.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Chōzuya}}]] The founding year of the shrine is unknown. According to the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}, an entry dated the 26th day of the 2nd month in the 9th year of Jōgan (867) mentions that the Hayashi Uji Shrine of Kawachi Province was recognized as a state shrine. Another entry from the 20th day of the 12th month in the 15th year of Jōgan (873) records the elevation of the enshrined deity Ame-no-Oshihi to the rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. During the {{ill|Sengoku period (Japan)|ja|戦国時代}}, the shrine was burned down by the forces of {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|ja|織田信長}}. After the extinction of the Tomobayashi clan, local residents reconstructed the shrine as a place to worship their {{ill|Ujigami|ja|産土神}}. In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the shrine's identification as Tomobayashi Uji Shrine was debated among {{ill|Kokugaku|lt=Kokugaku scholars|ja|国学}}. Despite inquiries by Edo officials, local village leaders at the time claimed it was a shrine dedicated to Gozu Tennō. By the late Edo period, the shrine became recognized as a site for worshiping the ancestral deity of the Ōtomo clan, influenced by the work of {{ill|Tomobayashi Mitsuhei|ja|伴林光平}}, a Kokugaku scholar from the village.Keita Endō, "Studies and Restoration of Shikinaisha: Investigation of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine" (First published in ''Shidai Nihonshi'', Vol. 5, 2002 / Collected in Endō, ''Formation of the Nihon Shoki and Related Materials'', Hanawa Shobō, 2015). In 1872 (Meiji 5), the shrine was designated as a village shrine. In 1932 (Shōwa 7), a survey commemorating the 50th anniversary of the {{ill|Imperial Rescript to Soldiers and Sailors|ja|軍人勅諭}} confirmed that this shrine is the only one in Japan dedicated to Michinoomi no Mikoto, the military deity of the Ōtomo clan. Consequently, it gained attention as the "Western Yasukuni Shrine." This designation heavily relied on the interpretation of Kokugaku scholar and historian {{ill|Ikuta namekine|ja|生田目経徳}}. Subsequent renovations and expansions led to the completion of new shrine buildings in 1940 (Shōwa 15), including the relocation of a {{ill|Chōzuya|ja|手水舎}} donated by {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}} in Tokyo. The shrine was elevated to the rank of prefectural shrine in 1942 (Shōwa 17). The shrine marker was inscribed by {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}}. == Grounds == * Main Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Offering Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Corridor Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940. * Gate (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940. * Shrine Office * Chōzuya (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Donated by Yasukuni Shrine in 1940; originally constructed in 1872. === Sub-Shrines === * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Formerly the reliquary hall of Sawada Elementary School (now Dōmyōji Elementary School). == Cultural Properties == === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022.Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 100, June 29, 2022. * Worship Hall and Offering Hall of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Gate and Corridor Fence of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Chōzuya of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Hall (Former Sawada Elementary School Reliquary Hall) - Registered June 29, 2022. == location == * Address: Hayashi 3-chome 6-30, Fujidera City, Osaka Prefecture == Access == * By rail: 10-minute walk northwest (approximately 800m) from {{ill|Hajinonosato Station|ja|土師ノ里駅}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線}}. == References == The shrine was elevated to the rank of prefectural shrine in 1942 (Shōwa 17). The shrine marker was inscribed by {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}}. == Grounds == * Main Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Offering Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Corridor Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940. * Gate (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940. * Shrine Office * Chōzuya (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Donated by Yasukuni Shrine in 1940; originally constructed in 1872. === Sub-Shrines === * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Formerly the reliquary hall of Sawada Elementary School (now Dōmyōji Elementary School). == Cultural Properties == === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022.Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 100, June 29, 2022. * Worship Hall and Offering Hall of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Gate and Corridor Fence of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Chōzuya of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Hall (Former Sawada Elementary School Reliquary Hall) - Registered June 29, 2022. == location == * Address: Hayashi 3-chome 6-30, Fujidera City, Osaka Prefecture == Access == * By rail: 10-minute walk northwest (approximately 800m) from {{ill|Hajinonosato Station|ja|土師ノ里駅}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == External links == {{Commonscat|Tomobayashiuji-jinja}} *[http://www.tomobayashiuji-jinja.jp/index.html Tomobayashi Uji Shrine] [[Category:Shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Fujidera]] [[Category:History of Fujidera]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Former prefectural shrines in Osaka]] [[Category:bump]] [[Category:Tomobayashi Shrine]][[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:Tomobayashi Shrine]][[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:deprecated qq open]]

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Tomobayashi Uji Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Tomobayashiuji-jinja, haiden.jpg|280px]]
Worship Hall | location = {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}{{ill|Fujidera City|ja|藤井寺市}} Hayashi 3-chome 6-30 | Latitude degrees = 34 | Latitude minutes = 34 | Latitude seconds = 32.649 | N/S = N | Longitude degrees = 135 | Longitude minutes = 36 | Longitude seconds = 40.439 | E/W = E | Map country code = JP | Map = Japan Osaka | Deities = {{ill|Takami-Musubi no Kami|ja|高皇産霊神}} | Shrine rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (minor)
Former {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|府社}} | consecration_year = Unknown | Main Hall = | Other names = | Pilgrimage sites = | festival = October 9 | Rituals = }} [[File:Tomobayashiuji-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Tomobayashi Uji Shrine''' (伴林氏神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Fujidera City|ja|藤井寺市}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|ja|大阪府}}, in the Hayashi area of the former Haishi-go, Shiki District, Kawachi Province. The shrine is listed as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} and previously held the rank of {{ill|Prefectural shrine|ja|府社}}. It was once referred to as the "Western {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}}." == Deities == * Primary deity - {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|高皇産霊神}} (たかむすひのかみ) * Secondary deities - {{ill|Michinoomi no Mikoto|ja|道臣命}} (みちのおみのみこと), {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天押日命}} (あめのおしひのみこと) The shrine's name suggests that it was originally established by the {{ill|Tomobayashi clan|ja|伴林氏}}, a branch of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大伴氏}}, to worship their ancestral deities. Takami-Musubi no Kami is identified as the fifth-generation ancestor of Ame-no-Oshihi, who in turn is the third-generation ancestor of Michinoomi no Mikoto. == History == [[File:Tomobayashiuji-jinja, chouzusha.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Chōzuya}}]] The founding year of the shrine is unknown. According to the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}, an entry dated the 26th day of the 2nd month in the 9th year of Jōgan (867) mentions that the Hayashi Uji Shrine of Kawachi Province was recognized as a state shrine. Another entry from the 20th day of the 12th month in the 15th year of Jōgan (873) records the elevation of the enshrined deity Ame-no-Oshihi to the rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. During the {{ill|Sengoku period (Japan)|ja|戦国時代}}, the shrine was burned down by the forces of {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|ja|織田信長}}. After the extinction of the Tomobayashi clan, local residents reconstructed the shrine as a place to worship their {{ill|Ujigami|ja|産土神}}. In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the shrine's identification as Tomobayashi Uji Shrine was debated among {{ill|Kokugaku|lt=Kokugaku scholars|ja|国学}}. Despite inquiries by Edo officials, local village leaders at the time claimed it was a shrine dedicated to Gozu Tennō. By the late Edo period, the shrine became recognized as a site for worshiping the ancestral deity of the Ōtomo clan, influenced by the work of {{ill|Tomobayashi Mitsuhei|ja|伴林光平}}, a Kokugaku scholar from the village.Keita Endō, "Studies and Restoration of Shikinaisha: Investigation of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine" (First published in ''Shidai Nihonshi'', Vol. 5, 2002 / Collected in Endō, ''Formation of the Nihon Shoki and Related Materials'', Hanawa Shobō, 2015). In 1872 (Meiji 5), the shrine was designated as a village shrine. In 1932 (Shōwa 7), a survey commemorating the 50th anniversary of the {{ill|Imperial Rescript to Soldiers and Sailors|ja|軍人勅諭}} confirmed that this shrine is the only one in Japan dedicated to Michinoomi no Mikoto, the military deity of the Ōtomo clan. Consequently, it gained attention as the "Western Yasukuni Shrine." This designation heavily relied on the interpretation of Kokugaku scholar and historian {{ill|Ikuta namekine|ja|生田目経徳}}. Subsequent renovations and expansions led to the completion of new shrine buildings in 1940 (Shōwa 15), including the relocation of a {{ill|Chōzuya|ja|手水舎}} donated by {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}} in Tokyo. The shrine was elevated to the rank of prefectural shrine in 1942 (Shōwa 17). The shrine marker was inscribed by {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}}. == Grounds == * Main Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Offering Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Corridor Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940. * Gate (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940. * Shrine Office * Chōzuya (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Donated by Yasukuni Shrine in 1940; originally constructed in 1872. === Sub-Shrines === * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Formerly the reliquary hall of Sawada Elementary School (now Dōmyōji Elementary School). == Cultural Properties == === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022.Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 100, June 29, 2022. * Worship Hall and Offering Hall of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Gate and Corridor Fence of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Chōzuya of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Hall (Former Sawada Elementary School Reliquary Hall) - Registered June 29, 2022. == location == * Address: Hayashi 3-chome 6-30, Fujidera City, Osaka Prefecture == Access == * By rail: 10-minute walk northwest (approximately 800m) from {{ill|Hajinonosato Station|ja|土師ノ里駅}} on the {{ill|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|ja|近鉄南大阪線}}. == References ==
