[[co:commons:Category:Tomobayashiuji-jinja]][[ja:伴林氏神社]] [[co:commons:Category:Tomobayashiuji-jinja]][[ja:伴林氏神社]] [[sn:commons:Category:Tomobayashiuji-jinja]][[ja:伴林氏神社]] {{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11381529]] Wikidata name: Tomobayashiuji-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:00, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Tomobayashi Uji Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Tomohayashi-no-uji-no-jinja (Shrine in Fujiidera).jpg | location = [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]][[:en:Fujiidera|Fujidera City]] Hayashi 3-chome 6-30 | Latitude degrees = 34 | Latitude minutes = 34 | Latitude seconds = 32.649 | N/S = N | Longitude degrees = 135 | Longitude minutes = 36 | Longitude seconds = 40.439 | E/W = E | Map country code = JP | Map = Japan Osaka | Deities = {{ill|Takami-Musubi no Kami|ja|高皇産霊神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takami-Musubi no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タカミムスビ|1=Takamimusubi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125400}} | Shrine Rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} (minor)
Former {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|府社|1=Metropolitan Shrine|WD=Q134917326}}{{efn|lt=Fu-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} | consecration_year = Unknown | Main Hall = | Other names = | Pilgrimage sites = | festival = October 9 | Rituals = | location = {{ill|Fujiidera|WD=Q634011}} | coordinates = {{Coord|34.57573583|N|135.61123306|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} [[File:Tomobayashiuji-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Tomobayashi Uji Shrine''' (伴林氏神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Fujiidera|Fujidera City]], [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], in the Hayashi area of the former Haishi-go, Shiki District, Kawachi Province. The shrine is listed as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} and previously held the Rank of {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|府社|1=Metropolitan Shrine|WD=Q134917326}}{{efn|lt=Fu-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. It was once referred to as the "Western [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]]." == Deities == * Primary deity - {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|高皇産霊神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamimusubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タカミムスビ}} (たかむすひのかみ) * Secondary deities - {{ill|Michinoomi no Mikoto|ja|道臣命|1=Michinoomi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Michinoomi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Michinoomi|qq=}} (みちのおみのみこと), {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天押日命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Oshihi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノオシヒ|1=Amanōshihi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10939616|1=Ama-no-Oshihi|2=ja|3=アメノオシヒ|4=zh|5=天忍日命}} (あめのおしひのみこと) The shrine's name suggests that it was originally established by the {{ill|Tomobayashi clan|ja|伴林氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomobayashi clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a branch of the [[:en:Ōtomo clan (ancient)|Ōtomo clan]], to worship their ancestral deities. Takami-Musubi no Kami is identified as the fifth-generation ancestor of Ame-no-Oshihi, who in turn is the third-generation ancestor of Michinoomi no Mikoto. == History == [[File:Tomobayashiuji-jinja, chouzusha.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Chōzuya}}]] The founding year of the shrine is unknown. According to the [[:en:Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]], an entry dated the 26th day of the 2nd month in the 9th year of Jōgan (867) mentions that the Hayashi Uji Shrine of Kawachi Province was recognized as a state shrine. Another entry from the 20th day of the 12th month in the 15th year of Jōgan (873) records the elevation of the enshrined deity Ame-no-Oshihi to the Rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. During the {{ill|Sengoku period (Japan)|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period (Japan)|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, the shrine was burned down by the forces of [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]]. After the extinction of the Tomobayashi clan, local residents reconstructed the shrine as a place to worship their [[:en:Ubusunagami|Ubusunagami]]. In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the shrine's identification as Tomobayashi Uji Shrine was debated among [[:en:Kokugaku|Kokugaku scholars]]. Despite inquiries by Edo officials, local village leaders at the time claimed it was a shrine dedicated to Gozu Tennō. By the late Edo period, the shrine became recognized as a site for worshiping the ancestral deity of the Ōtomo clan, influenced by the work of {{ill|Tomobayashi Mitsuhei|ja|伴林光平|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomobayashi Mitsuhei|1=Tomobayashi Mitsuhira|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6537004}}, a Kokugaku scholar from the village.Keita Endō, "Studies and Restoration of Shikinaisha: Investigation of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine" (First published in ''Shidai Nihonshi'', Vol. 5, 2002 / Collected in Endō, ''Formation of the Nihon Shoki and Related Materials'', Hanawa Shobō, 2015). In 1872 (Meiji 5), the shrine was designated as a village shrine. In 1932 (Shōwa 7), a survey commemorating the 50th anniversary of the [[:en:Imperial Rescript to Soldiers and Sailors|Imperial Rescript to Soldiers and Sailors]] confirmed that this shrine is the only one in Japan dedicated to Michinoomi no Mikoto, the military deity of the Ōtomo clan. Consequently, it gained attention as the "Western Yasukuni Shrine." This designation heavily relied on the interpretation of Kokugaku scholar and historian {{ill|Ikuta namekine|ja|生田目経徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikuta namekine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Subsequent renovations and expansions led to the Completion tier 6]] in Tokyo. The shrine was elevated to the Rank of prefectural shrine in 1942 (Shōwa 17). The shrine marker was inscribed by [[:en:Fumimaro Konoe|Fumimaro Konoe]]. == Grounds == * Main Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Offering Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Corridor Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940. * Gate (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940. * Shrine Office * Chōzuya (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Donated by Yasukuni Shrine in 1940; originally constructed in 1872. === Sub-Shrines === * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Formerly the reliquary hall of Sawada Elementary School (now Dōmyōji Elementary School). == Cultural Properties == === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022.Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 100, June 29, 2022. * Worship Hall and Offering Hall of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Gate and Corridor Fence of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Chōzuya of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Hall (Former Sawada Elementary School Reliquary Hall) - Registered June 29, 2022. == location == * Address: Hayashi 3-chome 6-30, Fujidera City, Osaka Prefecture == Access == * By rail: 10-minute walk northwest (approximately 800m) from [[:en:Hajinosato Station|Hajinonosato Station]] on the [[:en:Minami Osaka Line|Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line]]. == References == {{Reflist}} == External links == {{Commonscat|Tomobayashiuji-jinja}} *[http://www.tomobayashiuji-jinja.jp/index.html Tomobayashi Uji Shrine] {{translated page|ja|伴林氏神社|version=94918840|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:伴林氏神社]] {{Commons category|Tomobayashiuji-jinja}} {{wikidata link|Q11381529}} [[Category: of new shrine buildings in 1940 (Shōwa 15), including the relocation of a {{ill|Chōzuya|ja|手水舎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōzuya|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 手水|1=chōzuya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1069667}} donated by [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]] [[Category:Buildings in Fujiidera, Osaka]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Former Fu-sha in Osaka]] [[Category:History of Fujiidera, Osaka]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

The shrine is listed as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} and previously held the Rank of {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|府社|1=Metropolitan Shrine|WD=Q134917326}}{{efn|lt=Fu-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. It was once referred to as the "Western {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine}}." == Deities == * Primary deity - {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|高皇産霊神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamimusubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タカミムスビ}} (たかむすひのかみ) * Secondary deities - {{ill|Michinoomi no Mikoto|ja|道臣命|1=Michinoomi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Michinoomi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Michinoomi|qq=}} (みちのおみのみこと), {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天押日命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Oshihi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノオシヒ|1=Amanōshihi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10939616|1=Ama-no-Oshihi|2=ja|3=アメノオシヒ|4=zh|5=天忍日命}} (あめのおしひのみこと) The shrine's name suggests that it was originally established by the {{ill|Tomobayashi clan|ja|伴林氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomobayashi clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a branch of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|en|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|WD=Q17227938|lt=Ōtomo clan}}, to worship their ancestral deities. Takami-Musubi no Kami is identified as the fifth-generation ancestor of Ame-no-Oshihi, who in turn is the third-generation ancestor of Michinoomi no Mikoto. == History == [[File:Tomobayashiuji-jinja, chouzusha.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Chōzuya}}]] The founding year of the shrine is unknown. According to the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|WD=Q1052981|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}, an entry dated the 26th day of the 2nd month in the 9th year of Jōgan (867) mentions that the Hayashi Uji Shrine of Kawachi Province was recognized as a state shrine. Another entry from the 20th day of the 12th month in the 15th year of Jōgan (873) records the elevation of the enshrined deity Ame-no-Oshihi to the Rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. During the {{ill|Sengoku period (Japan)|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period (Japan)|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, the shrine was burned down by the forces of {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}}. After the extinction of the Tomobayashi clan, local residents reconstructed the shrine as a place to worship their {{ill|Ubusunagami|en|Ubusunagami|WD=Q1392097|lt=Ubusunagami}}. In the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the shrine's identification as Tomobayashi Uji Shrine was debated among {{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|WD=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku scholars}}. Despite inquiries by Edo officials, local village leaders at the time claimed it was a shrine dedicated to Gozu Tennō. By the late Edo period, the shrine became recognized as a site for worshiping the ancestral deity of the Ōtomo clan, influenced by the work of {{ill|Tomobayashi Mitsuhei|ja|伴林光平|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomobayashi Mitsuhei|1=Tomobayashi Mitsuhira|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6537004}}, a Kokugaku scholar from the village.Keita Endō, "Studies and Restoration of Shikinaisha: Investigation of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine" (First published in ''Shidai Nihonshi'', Vol. 5, 2002 / Collected in Endō, ''Formation of the Nihon Shoki and Related Materials'', Hanawa Shobō, 2015). In 1872 (Meiji 5), the shrine was designated as a village shrine. In 1932 (Shōwa 7), a survey commemorating the 50th anniversary of the {{ill|Imperial Rescript to Soldiers and Sailors|en|Imperial Rescript to Soldiers and Sailors|WD=Q3427667|lt=Imperial Rescript to Soldiers and Sailors}} confirmed that this shrine is the only one in Japan dedicated to Michinoomi no Mikoto, the military deity of the Ōtomo clan. Consequently, it gained attention as the "Western Yasukuni Shrine." This designation heavily relied on the interpretation of Kokugaku scholar and historian {{ill|Ikuta namekine|ja|生田目経徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikuta namekine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Subsequent renovations and expansions led to the Completion tier 6]] in Tokyo. The shrine was elevated to the Rank of prefectural shrine in 1942 (Shōwa 17). The shrine marker was inscribed by {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|en|Fumimaro Konoe|WD=Q315594|lt=Fumimaro Konoe}}. == Grounds == * Main Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Offering Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Corridor Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940. * Gate (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940. * Shrine Office * Chōzuya (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Donated by Yasukuni Shrine in 1940; originally constructed in 1872. === Sub-Shrines === * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Formerly the reliquary hall of Sawada Elementary School (now Dōmyōji Elementary School). == Cultural Properties == === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022.Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 100, June 29, 2022. * Worship Hall and Offering Hall of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Gate and Corridor Fence of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Chōzuya of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Hall (Former Sawada Elementary School Reliquary Hall) - Registered June 29, 2022. == location == * Address: Hayashi 3-chome 6-30, Fujidera City, Osaka Prefecture == Access == * By rail: 10-minute walk northwest (approximately 800m) from {{ill|Hajinosato Station|en|Hajinosato Station|WD=Q5640000|lt=Hajinonosato Station}} on the {{ill|Minami Osaka Line|en|Minami Osaka Line|WD=Q901342|lt=Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == External links == {{Commonscat|Tomobayashiuji-jinja}} *[http://www.tomobayashiuji-jinja.jp/index.html Tomobayashi Uji Shrine] {{translated page|ja|伴林氏神社|version=94918840|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:伴林氏神社]] {{Commons category|Tomobayashiuji-jinja}} {{wikidata link|Q11381529}} [[Category: of new shrine buildings in 1940 (Shōwa 15), including the relocation of a {{ill|Chōzuya|ja|手水舎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōzuya|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 手水|1=chōzuya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1069667}} donated by {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine}} [[Category:Buildings in Fujiidera, Osaka]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Former Fu-sha in Osaka]] [[Category:History of Fujiidera, Osaka]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed curly braces]]

Former {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|府社|1=Metropolitan Shrine|WD=Q134917326}}{{efn|lt=Fu-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} | consecration_year = Unknown | Main Hall = | Other names = | Pilgrimage sites = | festival = October 9 | Rituals = | location = {{ill|Fujiidera|WD=Q634011}} | coordinates = {{Coord|34.57573583|N|135.61123306|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} [[File:Tomobayashiuji-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Tomobayashi Uji Shrine''' (伴林氏神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Fujiidera|en|Fujiidera|WD=Q634011|lt=Fujidera City}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture}}, in the Hayashi area of the former Haishi-go, Shiki District, Kawachi Province. The shrine is listed as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} and previously held the Rank of {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|府社|1=Metropolitan Shrine|WD=Q134917326}}{{efn|lt=Fu-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. It was once referred to as the "Western {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine}}." == Deities == * Primary deity - {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|高皇産霊神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamimusubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タカミムスビ}} (たかむすひのかみ) * Secondary deities - {{ill|Michinoomi no Mikoto|ja|道臣命|1=Michinoomi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Michinoomi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Michinoomi|qq=}} (みちのおみのみこと), {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天押日命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Oshihi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノオシヒ|1=Amanōshihi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10939616|1=Ama-no-Oshihi|2=ja|3=アメノオシヒ|4=zh|5=天忍日命}} (あめのおしひのみこと) The shrine's name suggests that it was originally established by the {{ill|Tomobayashi clan|ja|伴林氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomobayashi clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a branch of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|en|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|WD=Q17227938|lt=Ōtomo clan}}, to worship their ancestral deities. Takami-Musubi no Kami is identified as the fifth-generation ancestor of Ame-no-Oshihi, who in turn is the third-generation ancestor of Michinoomi no Mikoto. == History == [[File:Tomobayashiuji-jinja, chouzusha.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Chōzuya}}]] The founding year of the shrine is unknown. According to the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|WD=Q1052981|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}, an entry dated the 26th day of the 2nd month in the 9th year of Jōgan (867) mentions that the Hayashi Uji Shrine of Kawachi Province was recognized as a state shrine. Another entry from the 20th day of the 12th month in the 15th year of Jōgan (873) records the elevation of the enshrined deity Ame-no-Oshihi to the Rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. During the {{ill|Sengoku period (Japan)|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period (Japan)|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, the shrine was burned down by the forces of {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}}. After the extinction of the Tomobayashi clan, local residents reconstructed the shrine as a place to worship their {{ill|Ubusunagami|en|Ubusunagami|WD=Q1392097|lt=Ubusunagami}}. In the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the shrine's identification as Tomobayashi Uji Shrine was debated among {{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|WD=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku scholars}}. Despite inquiries by Edo officials, local village leaders at the time claimed it was a shrine dedicated to Gozu Tennō. By the late Edo period, the shrine became recognized as a site for worshiping the ancestral deity of the Ōtomo clan, influenced by the work of {{ill|Tomobayashi Mitsuhei|ja|伴林光平|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomobayashi Mitsuhei|1=Tomobayashi Mitsuhira|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6537004}}, a Kokugaku scholar from the village.Keita Endō, "Studies and Restoration of Shikinaisha: Investigation of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine" (First published in ''Shidai Nihonshi'', Vol. 5, 2002 / Collected in Endō, ''Formation of the Nihon Shoki and Related Materials'', Hanawa Shobō, 2015). In 1872 (Meiji 5), the shrine was designated as a village shrine. In 1932 (Shōwa 7), a survey commemorating the 50th anniversary of the {{ill|Imperial Rescript to Soldiers and Sailors|en|Imperial Rescript to Soldiers and Sailors|WD=Q3427667|lt=Imperial Rescript to Soldiers and Sailors}} confirmed that this shrine is the only one in Japan dedicated to Michinoomi no Mikoto, the military deity of the Ōtomo clan. Consequently, it gained attention as the "Western Yasukuni Shrine." This designation heavily relied on the interpretation of Kokugaku scholar and historian {{ill|Ikuta namekine|ja|生田目経徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikuta namekine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Subsequent renovations and expansions led to the Completion tier 6]] in Tokyo. The shrine was elevated to the Rank of prefectural shrine in 1942 (Shōwa 17). The shrine marker was inscribed by {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|en|Fumimaro Konoe|WD=Q315594|lt=Fumimaro Konoe}}. == Grounds == * Main Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Offering Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Corridor Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940. * Gate (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940. * Shrine Office * Chōzuya (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Donated by Yasukuni Shrine in 1940; originally constructed in 1872. === Sub-Shrines === * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Formerly the reliquary hall of Sawada Elementary School (now Dōmyōji Elementary School). == Cultural Properties == === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022.Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 100, June 29, 2022. * Worship Hall and Offering Hall of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Gate and Corridor Fence of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Chōzuya of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Hall (Former Sawada Elementary School Reliquary Hall) - Registered June 29, 2022. == location == * Address: Hayashi 3-chome 6-30, Fujidera City, Osaka Prefecture == Access == * By rail: 10-minute walk northwest (approximately 800m) from {{ill|Hajinosato Station|en|Hajinosato Station|WD=Q5640000|lt=Hajinonosato Station}} on the {{ill|Minami Osaka Line|en|Minami Osaka Line|WD=Q901342|lt=Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == External links == {{Commonscat|Tomobayashiuji-jinja}} *[http://www.tomobayashiuji-jinja.jp/index.html Tomobayashi Uji Shrine] {{translated page|ja|伴林氏神社|version=94918840|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:伴林氏神社]] {{Commons category|Tomobayashiuji-jinja}} {{wikidata link|Q11381529}} [[Category: of new shrine buildings in 1940 (Shōwa 15), including the relocation of a {{ill|Chōzuya|ja|手水舎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōzuya|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 手水|1=chōzuya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1069667}} donated by {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine}} [[Category:Buildings in Fujiidera, Osaka]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Former Fu-sha in Osaka]] [[Category:History of Fujiidera, Osaka]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed curly braces]]

During the {{ill|Sengoku period (Japan)|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period (Japan)|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, the shrine was burned down by the forces of {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}}. After the extinction of the Tomobayashi clan, local residents reconstructed the shrine as a place to worship their {{ill|Ubusunagami|en|Ubusunagami|WD=Q1392097|lt=Ubusunagami}}. In the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the shrine's identification as Tomobayashi Uji Shrine was debated among {{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|WD=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku scholars}}. Despite inquiries by Edo officials, local village leaders at the time claimed it was a shrine dedicated to Gozu Tennō. By the late Edo period, the shrine became recognized as a site for worshiping the ancestral deity of the Ōtomo clan, influenced by the work of {{ill|Tomobayashi Mitsuhei|ja|伴林光平|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomobayashi Mitsuhei|1=Tomobayashi Mitsuhira|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6537004}}, a Kokugaku scholar from the village.Keita Endō, "Studies and Restoration of Shikinaisha: Investigation of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine" (First published in ''Shidai Nihonshi'', Vol. 5, 2002 / Collected in Endō, ''Formation of the Nihon Shoki and Related Materials'', Hanawa Shobō, 2015). In 1872 (Meiji 5), the shrine was designated as a village shrine. In 1932 (Shōwa 7), a survey commemorating the 50th anniversary of the {{ill|Imperial Rescript to Soldiers and Sailors|en|Imperial Rescript to Soldiers and Sailors|WD=Q3427667|lt=Imperial Rescript to Soldiers and Sailors}} confirmed that this shrine is the only one in Japan dedicated to Michinoomi no Mikoto, the military deity of the Ōtomo clan. Consequently, it gained attention as the "Western Yasukuni Shrine." This designation heavily relied on the interpretation of Kokugaku scholar and historian {{ill|Ikuta namekine|ja|生田目経徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikuta namekine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Subsequent renovations and expansions led to the Completion tier 6]] in Tokyo. The shrine was elevated to the Rank of prefectural shrine in 1942 (Shōwa 17). The shrine marker was inscribed by {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|en|Fumimaro Konoe|WD=Q315594|lt=Fumimaro Konoe}}. == Grounds == * Main Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Offering Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Corridor Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940. * Gate (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940. * Shrine Office * Chōzuya (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Donated by Yasukuni Shrine in 1940; originally constructed in 1872. === Sub-Shrines === * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Formerly the reliquary hall of Sawada Elementary School (now Dōmyōji Elementary School). == Cultural Properties == === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022.Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 100, June 29, 2022. * Worship Hall and Offering Hall of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Gate and Corridor Fence of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Chōzuya of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Hall (Former Sawada Elementary School Reliquary Hall) - Registered June 29, 2022. == location == * Address: Hayashi 3-chome 6-30, Fujidera City, Osaka Prefecture == Access == * By rail: 10-minute walk northwest (approximately 800m) from {{ill|Hajinosato Station|en|Hajinosato Station|WD=Q5640000|lt=Hajinonosato Station}} on the {{ill|Minami Osaka Line|en|Minami Osaka Line|WD=Q901342|lt=Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == External links == {{Commonscat|Tomobayashiuji-jinja}} *[http://www.tomobayashiuji-jinja.jp/index.html Tomobayashi Uji Shrine] {{translated page|ja|伴林氏神社|version=94918840|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:伴林氏神社]] {{Commons category|Tomobayashiuji-jinja}} {{wikidata link|Q11381529}} [[Category: of new shrine buildings in 1940 (Shōwa 15), including the relocation of a {{ill|Chōzuya|ja|手水舎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōzuya|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 手水|1=chōzuya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1069667}} donated by {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine}} [[Category:Buildings in Fujiidera, Osaka]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Former Fu-sha in Osaka]] [[Category:History of Fujiidera, Osaka]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed curly braces]] [[Category:Junior Fifth Rank]] [[Category:Senior Sixth Rank]] [[Category:Shikinai Shosha]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]]

Former {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|府社|1=Metropolitan Shrine|WD=Q134917326}}{{efn|lt=Fu-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} | consecration_year = Unknown | Main Hall = | Other names = | Pilgrimage sites = | festival = October 9 | Rituals = | location = {{ill|Fujiidera|WD=Q634011}} | coordinates = {{Coord|34.57573583|N|135.61123306|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} [[File:Tomobayashiuji-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Tomobayashi Uji Shrine''' (伴林氏神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Fujiidera|en|Fujiidera|WD=Q634011|lt=Fujidera City}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture}}, in the Hayashi area of the former Haishi-go, Shiki District, Kawachi Province. The shrine is listed as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} and previously held the Rank of {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|府社|1=Metropolitan Shrine|WD=Q134917326}}{{efn|lt=Fu-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. It was once referred to as the "Western {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine}}." == Deities == * Primary deity - {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|高皇産霊神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamimusubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タカミムスビ}} (たかむすひのかみ) * Secondary deities - {{ill|Michinoomi no Mikoto|ja|道臣命|1=Michinoomi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Michinoomi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Michinoomi|qq=}} (みちのおみのみこと), {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天押日命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Oshihi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノオシヒ|1=Amanōshihi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10939616|1=Ama-no-Oshihi|2=ja|3=アメノオシヒ|4=zh|5=天忍日命}} (あめのおしひのみこと) The shrine's name suggests that it was originally established by the {{ill|Tomobayashi clan|ja|伴林氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomobayashi clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a branch of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|en|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|WD=Q17227938|lt=Ōtomo clan}}, to worship their ancestral deities. Takami-Musubi no Kami is identified as the fifth-generation ancestor of Ame-no-Oshihi, who in turn is the third-generation ancestor of Michinoomi no Mikoto. == History == [[File:Tomobayashiuji-jinja, chouzusha.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Chōzuya}}]] The founding year of the shrine is unknown. According to the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|WD=Q1052981|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}, an entry dated the 26th day of the 2nd month in the 9th year of Jōgan (867) mentions that the Hayashi Uji Shrine of Kawachi Province was recognized as a state shrine. Another entry from the 20th day of the 12th month in the 15th year of Jōgan (873) records the elevation of the enshrined deity Ame-no-Oshihi to the Rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. During the {{ill|Sengoku period (Japan)|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period (Japan)|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, the shrine was burned down by the forces of {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}}. After the extinction of the Tomobayashi clan, local residents reconstructed the shrine as a place to worship their {{ill|Ubusunagami|en|Ubusunagami|WD=Q1392097|lt=Ubusunagami}}. In the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the shrine's identification as Tomobayashi Uji Shrine was debated among {{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|WD=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku scholars}}. Despite inquiries by Edo officials, local village leaders at the time claimed it was a shrine dedicated to Gozu Tennō. By the late Edo period, the shrine became recognized as a site for worshiping the ancestral deity of the Ōtomo clan, influenced by the work of {{ill|Tomobayashi Mitsuhei|ja|伴林光平|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomobayashi Mitsuhei|1=Tomobayashi Mitsuhira|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6537004}}, a Kokugaku scholar from the village.Keita Endō, "Studies and Restoration of Shikinaisha: Investigation of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine" (First published in ''Shidai Nihonshi'', Vol. 5, 2002 / Collected in Endō, ''Formation of the Nihon Shoki and Related Materials'', Hanawa Shobō, 2015). In 1872 (Meiji 5), the shrine was designated as a village shrine. In 1932 (Shōwa 7), a survey commemorating the 50th anniversary of the {{ill|Imperial Rescript to Soldiers and Sailors|en|Imperial Rescript to Soldiers and Sailors|WD=Q3427667|lt=Imperial Rescript to Soldiers and Sailors}} confirmed that this shrine is the only one in Japan dedicated to Michinoomi no Mikoto, the military deity of the Ōtomo clan. Consequently, it gained attention as the "Western Yasukuni Shrine." This designation heavily relied on the interpretation of Kokugaku scholar and historian {{ill|Ikuta namekine|ja|生田目経徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikuta namekine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Subsequent renovations and expansions led to the Completion tier 6]] in Tokyo. The shrine was elevated to the Rank of prefectural shrine in 1942 (Shōwa 17). The shrine marker was inscribed by {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|en|Fumimaro Konoe|WD=Q315594|lt=Fumimaro Konoe}}. == Grounds == * Main Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Offering Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Corridor Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940. * Gate (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940. * Shrine Office * Chōzuya (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Donated by Yasukuni Shrine in 1940; originally constructed in 1872. === Sub-Shrines === * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Formerly the reliquary hall of Sawada Elementary School (now Dōmyōji Elementary School). == Cultural Properties == === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022.Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 100, June 29, 2022. * Worship Hall and Offering Hall of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Gate and Corridor Fence of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Chōzuya of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Hall (Former Sawada Elementary School Reliquary Hall) - Registered June 29, 2022. == location == * Address: Hayashi 3-chome 6-30, Fujidera City, Osaka Prefecture == Access == * By rail: 10-minute walk northwest (approximately 800m) from {{ill|Hajinosato Station|en|Hajinosato Station|WD=Q5640000|lt=Hajinonosato Station}} on the {{ill|Minami Osaka Line|en|Minami Osaka Line|WD=Q901342|lt=Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == External links == {{Commonscat|Tomobayashiuji-jinja}} *[http://www.tomobayashiuji-jinja.jp/index.html Tomobayashi Uji Shrine] {{translated page|ja|伴林氏神社|version=94918840|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:伴林氏神社]] {{Commons category|Tomobayashiuji-jinja}} {{wikidata link|Q11381529}} [[Category: of new shrine buildings in 1940 (Shōwa 15), including the relocation of a {{ill|Chōzuya|ja|手水舎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōzuya|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 手水|1=chōzuya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1069667}} donated by {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine}} [[Category:Buildings in Fujiidera, Osaka]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Former Fu-sha in Osaka]] [[Category:History of Fujiidera, Osaka]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed curly braces]] [[Category:Junior Fifth Rank]] [[Category:Senior Sixth Rank]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]]

revision.text:
[[co:commons:Category:Tomobayashiuji-jinja]][[ja:伴林氏神社]] [[co:commons:Category:Tomobayashiuji-jinja]][[ja:伴林氏神社]] [[sn:commons:Category:Tomobayashiuji-jinja]][[ja:伴林氏神社]] { {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Tomobayashi Uji Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Tomohayashi-no-uji-no-jinja (Shrine in Fujiidera).jpg | location = {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture}}{{ill|Fujiidera|en|Fujiidera|WD=Q634011|lt=Fujidera City}} Hayashi 3-chome 6-30 | Latitude degrees = 34 | Latitude minutes = 34 | Latitude seconds = 32.649 | N/S = N | Longitude degrees = 135 | Longitude minutes = 36 | Longitude seconds = 40.439 | E/W = E | Map country code = JP | Map = Japan Osaka | Deities = {{ill|Takami-Musubi no Kami|ja|高皇産霊神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takami-Musubi no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タカミムスビ|1=Takamimusubi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125400}} | Shrine Rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} (minor)
Former {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|府社|1=Metropolitan Shrine|WD=Q134917326}}{{efn|lt=Fu-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} | consecration_year = Unknown | Main Hall = | Other names = | Pilgrimage sites = | festival = October 9 | Rituals = | location = {{ill|Fujiidera|WD=Q634011}} | coordinates = {{Coord|34.57573583|N|135.61123306|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} [[File:Tomobayashiuji-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Tomobayashi Uji Shrine''' (伴林氏神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Fujiidera|en|Fujiidera|WD=Q634011|lt=Fujidera City}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture}}, in the Hayashi area of the former Haishi-go, Shiki District, Kawachi Province. The shrine is listed as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} and previously held the Rank of {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|府社|1=Metropolitan Shrine|WD=Q134917326}}{{efn|lt=Fu-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. It was once referred to as the "Western {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine}}." == Deities == * Primary deity - {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|高皇産霊神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamimusubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タカミムスビ}} (たかむすひのかみ) * Secondary deities - {{ill|Michinoomi no Mikoto|ja|道臣命|1=Michinoomi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Michinoomi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Michinoomi|qq=}} (みちのおみのみこと), {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天押日命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Oshihi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノオシヒ|1=Amanōshihi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10939616|1=Ama-no-Oshihi|2=ja|3=アメノオシヒ|4=zh|5=天忍日命}} (あめのおしひのみこと) The shrine's name suggests that it was originally established by the {{ill|Tomobayashi clan|ja|伴林氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomobayashi clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a branch of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|en|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|WD=Q17227938|lt=Ōtomo clan}}, to worship their ancestral deities. Takami-Musubi no Kami is identified as the fifth-generation ancestor of Ame-no-Oshihi, who in turn is the third-generation ancestor of Michinoomi no Mikoto. == History == [[File:Tomobayashiuji-jinja, chouzusha.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Chōzuya}}]] The founding year of the shrine is unknown. According to the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|WD=Q1052981|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}, an entry dated the 26th day of the 2nd month in the 9th year of Jōgan (867) mentions that the Hayashi Uji Shrine of Kawachi Province was recognized as a state shrine. Another entry from the 20th day of the 12th month in the 15th year of Jōgan (873) records the elevation of the enshrined deity Ame-no-Oshihi to the Rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. During the {{ill|Sengoku period (Japan)|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period (Japan)|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, the shrine was burned down by the forces of {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}}. After the extinction of the Tomobayashi clan, local residents reconstructed the shrine as a place to worship their {{ill|Ubusunagami|en|Ubusunagami|WD=Q1392097|lt=Ubusunagami}}. In the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the shrine's identification as Tomobayashi Uji Shrine was debated among {{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|WD=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku scholars}}. Despite inquiries by Edo officials, local village leaders at the time claimed it was a shrine dedicated to Gozu Tennō. By the late Edo period, the shrine became recognized as a site for worshiping the ancestral deity of the Ōtomo clan, influenced by the work of {{ill|Tomobayashi Mitsuhei|ja|伴林光平|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomobayashi Mitsuhei|1=Tomobayashi Mitsuhira|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6537004}}, a Kokugaku scholar from the village.Keita Endō, "Studies and Restoration of Shikinaisha: Investigation of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine" (First published in ''Shidai Nihonshi'', Vol. 5, 2002 / Collected in Endō, ''Formation of the Nihon Shoki and Related Materials'', Hanawa Shobō, 2015). In 1872 (Meiji 5), the shrine was designated as a village shrine. In 1932 (Shōwa 7), a survey commemorating the 50th anniversary of the {{ill|Imperial Rescript to Soldiers and Sailors|en|Imperial Rescript to Soldiers and Sailors|WD=Q3427667|lt=Imperial Rescript to Soldiers and Sailors}} confirmed that this shrine is the only one in Japan dedicated to Michinoomi no Mikoto, the military deity of the Ōtomo clan. Consequently, it gained attention as the "Western Yasukuni Shrine." This designation heavily relied on the interpretation of Kokugaku scholar and historian {{ill|Ikuta namekine|ja|生田目経徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikuta namekine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Subsequent renovations and expansions led to the Completion tier 6]] in Tokyo. The shrine was elevated to the Rank of prefectural shrine in 1942 (Shōwa 17). The shrine marker was inscribed by {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|en|Fumimaro Konoe|WD=Q315594|lt=Fumimaro Konoe}}. == Grounds == * Main Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Offering Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Corridor Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940. * Gate (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940. * Shrine Office * Chōzuya (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Donated by Yasukuni Shrine in 1940; originally constructed in 1872. === Sub-Shrines === * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Formerly the reliquary hall of Sawada Elementary School (now Dōmyōji Elementary School). == Cultural Properties == === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022.Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 100, June 29, 2022. * Worship Hall and Offering Hall of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Gate and Corridor Fence of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Chōzuya of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Hall (Former Sawada Elementary School Reliquary Hall) - Registered June 29, 2022. == location == * Address: Hayashi 3-chome 6-30, Fujidera City, Osaka Prefecture == Access == * By rail: 10-minute walk northwest (approximately 800m) from {{ill|Hajinosato Station|en|Hajinosato Station|WD=Q5640000|lt=Hajinonosato Station}} on the {{ill|Minami Osaka Line|en|Minami Osaka Line|WD=Q901342|lt=Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == External links == {{Commonscat|Tomobayashiuji-jinja}} *[http://www.tomobayashiuji-jinja.jp/index.html Tomobayashi Uji Shrine] {{translated page|ja|伴林氏神社|version=94918840|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:伴林氏神社]] {{Commons category|Tomobayashiuji-jinja}} {{wikidata link|Q11381529}} [[Category: of new shrine buildings in 1940 (Shōwa 15), including the relocation of a {{ill|Chōzuya|ja|手水舎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōzuya|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 手水|1=chōzuya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1069667}} donated by {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine}} [[Category:Buildings in Fujiidera, Osaka]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Former Fu-sha in Osaka]] [[Category:History of Fujiidera, Osaka]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed curly braces]] [[Category:Junior Fifth Rank]] [[Category:Senior Sixth Rank]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]]

[[co:commons:Category:Tomobayashiuji-jinja]][[ja:伴林氏神社]] [[co:commons:Category:Tomobayashiuji-jinja]][[ja:伴林氏神社]] [[sn:commons:Category:Tomobayashiuji-jinja]][[ja:伴林氏神社]] { {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Tomobayashi Uji Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Tomohayashi-no-uji-no-jinja (Shrine in Fujiidera).jpg | location = {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}}{{ill|Fujiidera|en|Fujiidera|WD=Q634011|lt=Fujidera City|1=Fujiidera}} Hayashi 3-chome 6-30 | Latitude degrees = 34 | Latitude minutes = 34 | Latitude seconds = 32.649 | N/S = N | Longitude degrees = 135 | Longitude minutes = 36 | Longitude seconds = 40.439 | E/W = E | Map country code = JP | Map = Japan Osaka | Deities = {{ill|Takami-Musubi no Kami|ja|高皇産霊神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takami-Musubi no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タカミムスビ|1=Takamimusubi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125400}} | Shrine Rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} (minor)
Former {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|府社|1=Fu-sha|WD=Q134917326}}{{efn|lt=Fu-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} | consecration_year = Unknown | Main Hall = | Other names = | Pilgrimage sites = | festival = October 9 | Rituals = | location = {{ill|Fujiidera|WD=Q634011|1=Fujiidera}} | coordinates = {{Coord|34.57573583|N|135.61123306|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} [[File:Tomobayashiuji-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Tomobayashi Uji Shrine''' (伴林氏神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Fujiidera|en|Fujiidera|WD=Q634011|lt=Fujidera City|1=Fujiidera}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}}, in the Hayashi area of the former Haishi-go, Shiki District, Kawachi Province. The shrine is listed as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} and previously held the Rank of {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|府社|1=Fu-sha|WD=Q134917326}}{{efn|lt=Fu-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. It was once referred to as the "Western {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine|1=Yasukuni Shrine}}." == Deities == * Primary deity - {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|高皇産霊神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamimusubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タカミムスビ}} (たかむすひのかみ) * Secondary deities - {{ill|Michinoomi no Mikoto|ja|道臣命|1=Michinoomi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Michinoomi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Michinoomi|qq=}} (みちのおみのみこと), {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天押日命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Oshihi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノオシヒ|1=Ama-no-Oshihi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10939616|1=Ama-no-Oshihi|2=ja|3=アメノオシヒ|4=zh|5=天忍日命}} (あめのおしひのみこと) The shrine's name suggests that it was originally established by the {{ill|Tomobayashi clan|ja|伴林氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomobayashi clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a branch of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|en|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|WD=Q17227938|lt=Ōtomo clan|1=Ōtomo clan (ancient)}}, to worship their ancestral deities. Takami-Musubi no Kami is identified as the fifth-generation ancestor of Ame-no-Oshihi, who in turn is the third-generation ancestor of Michinoomi no Mikoto. == History == [[File:Tomobayashiuji-jinja, chouzusha.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Chōzuya}}]] The founding year of the shrine is unknown. According to the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|WD=Q1052981|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}, an entry dated the 26th day of the 2nd month in the 9th year of Jōgan (867) mentions that the Hayashi Uji Shrine of Kawachi Province was recognized as a state shrine. Another entry from the 20th day of the 12th month in the 15th year of Jōgan (873) records the elevation of the enshrined deity Ame-no-Oshihi to the Rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. During the {{ill|Sengoku period (Japan)|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period (Japan)|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, the shrine was burned down by the forces of {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga|1=Oda Nobunaga}}. After the extinction of the Tomobayashi clan, local residents reconstructed the shrine as a place to worship their {{ill|Ubusunagami|en|Ubusunagami|WD=Q1392097|lt=Ubusunagami|1=Ubusunagami}}. In the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the shrine's identification as Tomobayashi Uji Shrine was debated among {{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|WD=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku scholars|1=Kokugaku}}. Despite inquiries by Edo officials, local village leaders at the time claimed it was a shrine dedicated to Gozu Tennō. By the late Edo period, the shrine became recognized as a site for worshiping the ancestral deity of the Ōtomo clan, influenced by the work of {{ill|Tomobayashi Mitsuhei|ja|伴林光平|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomobayashi Mitsuhei|1=Tomobayashi Mitsuhira|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6537004}}, a Kokugaku scholar from the village.Keita Endō, "Studies and Restoration of Shikinaisha: Investigation of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine" (First published in ''Shidai Nihonshi'', Vol. 5, 2002 / Collected in Endō, ''Formation of the Nihon Shoki and Related Materials'', Hanawa Shobō, 2015). In 1872 (Meiji 5), the shrine was designated as a village shrine. In 1932 (Shōwa 7), a survey commemorating the 50th anniversary of the {{ill|Imperial Rescript to Soldiers and Sailors|en|Imperial Rescript to Soldiers and Sailors|WD=Q3427667|lt=Imperial Rescript to Soldiers and Sailors|1=Imperial Rescript to Soldiers and Sailors}} confirmed that this shrine is the only one in Japan dedicated to Michinoomi no Mikoto, the military deity of the Ōtomo clan. Consequently, it gained attention as the "Western Yasukuni Shrine." This designation heavily relied on the interpretation of Kokugaku scholar and historian {{ill|Ikuta namekine|ja|生田目経徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikuta namekine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Subsequent renovations and expansions led to the Completion tier 6]] in Tokyo. The shrine was elevated to the Rank of prefectural shrine in 1942 (Shōwa 17). The shrine marker was inscribed by {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|en|Fumimaro Konoe|WD=Q315594|lt=Fumimaro Konoe|1=Fumimaro Konoe}}. == Grounds == * Main Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Offering Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Corridor Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940. * Gate (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940. * Shrine Office * Chōzuya (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Donated by Yasukuni Shrine in 1940; originally constructed in 1872. === Sub-Shrines === * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Formerly the reliquary hall of Sawada Elementary School (now Dōmyōji Elementary School). == Cultural Properties == === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022.Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 100, June 29, 2022. * Worship Hall and Offering Hall of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Gate and Corridor Fence of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Chōzuya of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Hall (Former Sawada Elementary School Reliquary Hall) - Registered June 29, 2022. == location == * Address: Hayashi 3-chome 6-30, Fujidera City, Osaka Prefecture == Access == * By rail: 10-minute walk northwest (approximately 800m) from {{ill|Hajinosato Station|en|Hajinosato Station|WD=Q5640000|lt=Hajinonosato Station|1=Hajinosato Station}} on the {{ill|Minami Osaka Line|en|Minami Osaka Line|WD=Q901342|lt=Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|1=Minami Osaka Line}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == External links == {{Commonscat|Tomobayashiuji-jinja}} *[http://www.tomobayashiuji-jinja.jp/index.html Tomobayashi Uji Shrine] {{translated page|ja|伴林氏神社|version=94918840|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:伴林氏神社]] {{Commons category|Tomobayashiuji-jinja}} {{wikidata link|Q11381529}} [[Category: of new shrine buildings in 1940 (Shōwa 15), including the relocation of a {{ill|Chōzuya|ja|手水舎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōzuya|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 手水|1=Chōzuya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1069667}} donated by {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine|1=Yasukuni Shrine}} [[Category:Buildings in Fujiidera, Osaka]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Former Fu-sha in Osaka]] [[Category:History of Fujiidera, Osaka]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed curly braces]] [[Category:Junior Fifth Rank]] [[Category:Senior Sixth Rank]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]] [[Category:pages]]

[[co:commons:Category:Tomobayashiuji-jinja]][[ja:伴林氏神社]] [[co:commons:Category:Tomobayashiuji-jinja]][[ja:伴林氏神社]] [[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Tomobayashiuji-jinja]][[ja:伴林氏神社]] { {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Tomobayashi Uji Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Tomohayashi-no-uji-no-jinja (Shrine in Fujiidera).jpg | location = {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}}{{ill|Fujiidera|en|Fujiidera|WD=Q634011|lt=Fujidera City|1=Fujiidera}} Hayashi 3-chome 6-30 | Latitude degrees = 34 | Latitude minutes = 34 | Latitude seconds = 32.649 | N/S = N | Longitude degrees = 135 | Longitude minutes = 36 | Longitude seconds = 40.439 | E/W = E | Map country code = JP | Map = Japan Osaka | Deities = {{ill|Takami-Musubi no Kami|ja|高皇産霊神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takami-Musubi no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タカミムスビ|1=Takamimusubi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125400}} | Shrine Rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} (minor)
Former {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|府社|1=Fu-sha|WD=Q134917326}}{{efn|lt=Fu-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} | consecration_year = Unknown | Main Hall = | Other names = | Pilgrimage sites = | festival = October 9 | Rituals = | location = {{ill|Fujiidera|WD=Q634011|1=Fujiidera}} | coordinates = {{Coord|34.57573583|N|135.61123306|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} [[File:Tomobayashiuji-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Tomobayashi Uji Shrine''' (伴林氏神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Fujiidera|en|Fujiidera|WD=Q634011|lt=Fujidera City|1=Fujiidera}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}}, in the Hayashi area of the former Haishi-go, Shiki District, Kawachi Province. The shrine is listed as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} and previously held the Rank of {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|府社|1=Fu-sha|WD=Q134917326}}{{efn|lt=Fu-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. It was once referred to as the "Western {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine|1=Yasukuni Shrine}}." == Deities == * Primary deity - {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|高皇産霊神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamimusubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タカミムスビ}} (たかむすひのかみ) * Secondary deities - {{ill|Michinoomi no Mikoto|ja|道臣命|1=Michinoomi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Michinoomi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Michinoomi|qq=}} (みちのおみのみこと), {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天押日命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Oshihi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノオシヒ|1=Ama-no-Oshihi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10939616|1=Ama-no-Oshihi|2=ja|3=アメノオシヒ|4=zh|5=天忍日命}} (あめのおしひのみこと) The shrine's name suggests that it was originally established by the {{ill|Tomobayashi clan|ja|伴林氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomobayashi clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a branch of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|en|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|WD=Q17227938|lt=Ōtomo clan|1=Ōtomo clan (ancient)}}, to worship their ancestral deities. Takami-Musubi no Kami is identified as the fifth-generation ancestor of Ame-no-Oshihi, who in turn is the third-generation ancestor of Michinoomi no Mikoto. == History == [[File:Tomobayashiuji-jinja, chouzusha.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Chōzuya}}]] The founding year of the shrine is unknown. According to the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|WD=Q1052981|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}, an entry dated the 26th day of the 2nd month in the 9th year of Jōgan (867) mentions that the Hayashi Uji Shrine of Kawachi Province was recognized as a state shrine. Another entry from the 20th day of the 12th month in the 15th year of Jōgan (873) records the elevation of the enshrined deity Ame-no-Oshihi to the Rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. During the {{ill|Sengoku period (Japan)|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period (Japan)|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, the shrine was burned down by the forces of {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga|1=Oda Nobunaga}}. After the extinction of the Tomobayashi clan, local residents reconstructed the shrine as a place to worship their {{ill|Ubusunagami|en|Ubusunagami|WD=Q1392097|lt=Ubusunagami|1=Ubusunagami}}. In the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the shrine's identification as Tomobayashi Uji Shrine was debated among {{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|WD=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku scholars|1=Kokugaku}}. Despite inquiries by Edo officials, local village leaders at the time claimed it was a shrine dedicated to Gozu Tennō. By the late Edo period, the shrine became recognized as a site for worshiping the ancestral deity of the Ōtomo clan, influenced by the work of {{ill|Tomobayashi Mitsuhei|ja|伴林光平|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomobayashi Mitsuhei|1=Tomobayashi Mitsuhira|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6537004}}, a Kokugaku scholar from the village.Keita Endō, "Studies and Restoration of Shikinaisha: Investigation of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine" (First published in ''Shidai Nihonshi'', Vol. 5, 2002 / Collected in Endō, ''Formation of the Nihon Shoki and Related Materials'', Hanawa Shobō, 2015). In 1872 (Meiji 5), the shrine was designated as a village shrine. In 1932 (Shōwa 7), a survey commemorating the 50th anniversary of the {{ill|Imperial Rescript to Soldiers and Sailors|en|Imperial Rescript to Soldiers and Sailors|WD=Q3427667|lt=Imperial Rescript to Soldiers and Sailors|1=Imperial Rescript to Soldiers and Sailors}} confirmed that this shrine is the only one in Japan dedicated to Michinoomi no Mikoto, the military deity of the Ōtomo clan. Consequently, it gained attention as the "Western Yasukuni Shrine." This designation heavily relied on the interpretation of Kokugaku scholar and historian {{ill|Ikuta namekine|ja|生田目経徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikuta namekine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Subsequent renovations and expansions led to the Completion tier 6]] in Tokyo. The shrine was elevated to the Rank of prefectural shrine in 1942 (Shōwa 17). The shrine marker was inscribed by {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|en|Fumimaro Konoe|WD=Q315594|lt=Fumimaro Konoe|1=Fumimaro Konoe}}. == Grounds == * Main Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Offering Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Corridor Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940. * Gate (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940. * Shrine Office * Chōzuya (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Donated by Yasukuni Shrine in 1940; originally constructed in 1872. === Sub-Shrines === * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Formerly the reliquary hall of Sawada Elementary School (now Dōmyōji Elementary School). == Cultural Properties == === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022.Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 100, June 29, 2022. * Worship Hall and Offering Hall of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Gate and Corridor Fence of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Chōzuya of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Hall (Former Sawada Elementary School Reliquary Hall) - Registered June 29, 2022. == location == * Address: Hayashi 3-chome 6-30, Fujidera City, Osaka Prefecture == Access == * By rail: 10-minute walk northwest (approximately 800m) from {{ill|Hajinosato Station|en|Hajinosato Station|WD=Q5640000|lt=Hajinonosato Station|1=Hajinosato Station}} on the {{ill|Minami Osaka Line|en|Minami Osaka Line|WD=Q901342|lt=Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|1=Minami Osaka Line}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == External links == {{Commonscat|Tomobayashiuji-jinja}} *[http://www.tomobayashiuji-jinja.jp/index.html Tomobayashi Uji Shrine] {{translated page|ja|伴林氏神社|version=94918840|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:伴林氏神社]] {{Commons category|Tomobayashiuji-jinja}} {{wikidata link|Q11381529}} [[Category: of new shrine buildings in 1940 (Shōwa 15), including the relocation of a {{ill|Chōzuya|ja|手水舎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōzuya|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 手水|1=Chōzuya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1069667}} donated by {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine|1=Yasukuni Shrine}} [[Category:Buildings in Fujiidera, Osaka]] [[Category:Former Fu-sha in Osaka]] [[Category:History of Fujiidera, Osaka]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed curly braces]] [[Category:Junior Fifth Rank]] [[Category:Senior Sixth Rank]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]] [[Category:pages]]

[[co:commons:Category:Tomobayashiuji-jinja]][[ja:伴林氏神社]] [[co:commons:Category:Tomobayashiuji-jinja]][[ja:伴林氏神社]] [[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Tomobayashiuji-jinja]][[ja:伴林氏神社]] { {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Tomobayashi Uji Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Tomohayashi-no-uji-no-jinja (Shrine in Fujiidera).jpg | location = {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}}{{ill|Fujiidera|en|Fujiidera|WD=Q634011|lt=Fujidera City|1=Fujiidera}} Hayashi 3-chome 6-30 | Latitude degrees = 34 | Latitude minutes = 34 | Latitude seconds = 32.649 | N/S = N | Longitude degrees = 135 | Longitude minutes = 36 | Longitude seconds = 40.439 | E/W = E | Map country code = JP | Map = Japan Osaka | Deities = {{ill|Takami-Musubi no Kami|ja|高皇産霊神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takami-Musubi no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タカミムスビ|1=Takamimusubi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125400}} | Shrine Rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} (minor)
Former {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|府社|1=Fu-sha|WD=Q134917326}}{{efn|lt=Fu-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} | consecration_year = Unknown | Main Hall = | Other names = | Pilgrimage sites = | festival = October 9 | Rituals = | location = {{ill|Fujiidera|WD=Q634011|1=Fujiidera}} | coordinates = {{Coord|34.57573583|N|135.61123306|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} [[File:Tomobayashiuji-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Tomobayashi Uji Shrine''' (伴林氏神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Fujiidera|en|Fujiidera|WD=Q634011|lt=Fujidera City|1=Fujiidera}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}}, in the Hayashi area of the former Haishi-go, Shiki District, Kawachi Province. The shrine is listed as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} and previously held the Rank of {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Prefectural shrine|ja|府社|1=Fu-sha|WD=Q134917326}}{{efn|lt=Fu-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. It was once referred to as the "Western {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine|1=Yasukuni Shrine}}." == Deities == * Primary deity - {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|高皇産霊神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamimusubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タカミムスビ}} (たかむすひのかみ) * Secondary deities - {{ill|Michinoomi no Mikoto|ja|道臣命|1=Michinoomi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Michinoomi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Michinoomi|qq=}} (みちのおみのみこと), {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihi|ja|天押日命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Oshihi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノオシヒ|1=Ama-no-Oshihi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10939616|1=Ama-no-Oshihi|2=ja|3=アメノオシヒ|4=zh|5=天忍日命}} (あめのおしひのみこと) The shrine's name suggests that it was originally established by the {{ill|Tomobayashi clan|ja|伴林氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomobayashi clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a branch of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|en|Ōtomo clan (ancient)|WD=Q17227938|lt=Ōtomo clan|1=Ōtomo clan (ancient)}}, to worship their ancestral deities. Takami-Musubi no Kami is identified as the fifth-generation ancestor of Ame-no-Oshihi, who in turn is the third-generation ancestor of Michinoomi no Mikoto. == History == [[File:Tomobayashiuji-jinja, chouzusha.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Chōzuya}}]] The founding year of the shrine is unknown. According to the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|WD=Q1052981|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}, an entry dated the 26th day of the 2nd month in the 9th year of Jōgan (867) mentions that the Hayashi Uji Shrine of Kawachi Province was recognized as a state shrine. Another entry from the 20th day of the 12th month in the 15th year of Jōgan (873) records the elevation of the enshrined deity Ame-no-Oshihi to the Rank of Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade. During the {{ill|Sengoku period (Japan)|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period (Japan)|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, the shrine was burned down by the forces of {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga|1=Oda Nobunaga}}. After the extinction of the Tomobayashi clan, local residents reconstructed the shrine as a place to worship their {{ill|Ubusunagami|en|Ubusunagami|WD=Q1392097|lt=Ubusunagami|1=Ubusunagami}}. In the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the shrine's identification as Tomobayashi Uji Shrine was debated among {{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|WD=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku scholars|1=Kokugaku}}. Despite inquiries by Edo officials, local village leaders at the time claimed it was a shrine dedicated to Gozu Tennō. By the late Edo period, the shrine became recognized as a site for worshiping the ancestral deity of the Ōtomo clan, influenced by the work of {{ill|Tomobayashi Mitsuhei|ja|伴林光平|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomobayashi Mitsuhei|1=Tomobayashi Mitsuhira|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6537004}}, a Kokugaku scholar from the village.Keita Endō, "Studies and Restoration of Shikinaisha: Investigation of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine" (First published in ''Shidai Nihonshi'', Vol. 5, 2002 / Collected in Endō, ''Formation of the Nihon Shoki and Related Materials'', Hanawa Shobō, 2015). In 1872 (Meiji 5), the shrine was designated as a village shrine. In 1932 (Shōwa 7), a survey commemorating the 50th anniversary of the {{ill|Imperial Rescript to Soldiers and Sailors|en|Imperial Rescript to Soldiers and Sailors|WD=Q3427667|lt=Imperial Rescript to Soldiers and Sailors|1=Imperial Rescript to Soldiers and Sailors}} confirmed that this shrine is the only one in Japan dedicated to Michinoomi no Mikoto, the military deity of the Ōtomo clan. Consequently, it gained attention as the "Western Yasukuni Shrine." This designation heavily relied on the interpretation of Kokugaku scholar and historian {{ill|Ikuta namekine|ja|生田目経徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikuta namekine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Subsequent renovations and expansions led to the Completion tier 6]] in Tokyo. The shrine was elevated to the Rank of prefectural shrine in 1942 (Shōwa 17). The shrine marker was inscribed by {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|en|Fumimaro Konoe|WD=Q315594|lt=Fumimaro Konoe|1=Fumimaro Konoe}}. == Grounds == * Main Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Offering Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Rebuilt in 1940. * Corridor Fence (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940. * Gate (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Built in 1940. * Shrine Office * Chōzuya (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Donated by Yasukuni Shrine in 1940; originally constructed in 1872. === Sub-Shrines === * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) - Formerly the reliquary hall of Sawada Elementary School (now Dōmyōji Elementary School). == Cultural Properties == === Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === * Main Hall of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022.Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Notification No. 100, June 29, 2022. * Worship Hall and Offering Hall of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Gate and Corridor Fence of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Chōzuya of Tomobayashi Uji Shrine - Registered June 29, 2022. * Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine Hall (Former Sawada Elementary School Reliquary Hall) - Registered June 29, 2022. == location == * Address: Hayashi 3-chome 6-30, Fujidera City, Osaka Prefecture == Access == * By rail: 10-minute walk northwest (approximately 800m) from {{ill|Hajinosato Station|en|Hajinosato Station|WD=Q5640000|lt=Hajinonosato Station|1=Hajinosato Station}} on the {{ill|Minami Osaka Line|en|Minami Osaka Line|WD=Q901342|lt=Kintetsu Minami-Osaka Line|1=Minami Osaka Line}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == External links == {{Commonscat|Tomobayashiuji-jinja}} *[http://www.tomobayashiuji-jinja.jp/index.html Tomobayashi Uji Shrine] {{translated page|ja|伴林氏神社|version=94918840|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:伴林氏神社]] {{Commons category|Tomobayashiuji-jinja}} {{wikidata link|Q11381529}} [[Category: of new shrine buildings in 1940 (Shōwa 15), including the relocation of a {{ill|Chōzuya|ja|手水舎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōzuya|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 手水|1=Chōzuya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1069667}} donated by {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine|1=Yasukuni Shrine}} [[Category:Buildings and structures in Fujiidera, Osaka]] [[Category:Former Fu-sha in Osaka]] [[Category:History of Fujiidera, Osaka]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed curly braces]] [[Category:Junior Fifth Rank]] [[Category:Senior Sixth Rank]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]]
